The shorter output array thing is true for most of the functions. For example, if you call a simple moving average with a period of 10, your output will be 9 bars shorter. There's really no way around it.

I understand the theory of it and agree that it might be a problem, but I'm not yet convinced that this would actually be a common situation--it seems that it would require the task in question to be pegged at 100% CPU load continuously and to never block *ever*.   Given all the variances on a typical network, it seems like this would be pretty hard to achieve except in carefully controlled tests.  And even if that did happen, it seems like you'd have a much more serious problem on your hands related to the scaling of whatever thing you were running. 

I could be wrong though.  I just don't know.   How are other frameworks dealing with this?   How do programs that use threads deal with this?     

All things equal, I don't want to completely discount the performance overhead of constantly doing everything on the event loop by doing away with eager read/write.    I could possibly envision some kind of hybrid approach that allows a certain small number of eager reads to take place first before blocking on the event loop.   That might allow more normal workloads to take advantage of eager read/write while forcing the extreme case to more properly schedule itself.

Some comments on the example.... one danger of mixing threads and curio cancellation is that threads can't actually be cancelled (there is no mechanism for doing it).   Thus, if some operation is launched in a thread (e.g., using `abide()`) and a cancellation is issued, the underlying operation will still run to an eventual completion.   The result will end up getting lost to the sands of time.   I don't really know any workaround to this due to inherent limitations in the threading library.  

That said, pretty sure the bug here is due to a callback remaining attached to the `Future` that's still attached to the thread. It's being triggered at a point in time where Curio has already torn down its notification socket.   Should be fixable with the understanding that the result from the thread is still going to be lost.

This is the answer I received from Micropelt, which was very informative to me:
 - the Micropelt device supports only RORG 0xA5 and no ADT
 - in EnOcean, the sender address can be either a Unique ID (manufacturer chip-ID) or an address based on the Base-ID
 - the Base-ID can be set and read by COMMAN_COMMAND CO_WR_IDBASE (07) and CO_RD_IDBASE (08) and must be in range 0xFF800000-0xFFFFFF80 with the last 7 bits set to zero
 - these seven bits are used to emulate 128 IDs and are usually incremented for each device learnt, so one module can emulate 128 senders using the Base-ID
 - the actuator learns this unique Sender-ID during teach-in and uses this for addressing

The problem in my test described above was, that I used the sender address 0x17111976, which is outside the valid Base-ID range.

So, I see two possible approaches for this enocean library:
 1. the callback specified above, which leaves the user the obligation to determine the address (or the offset to the base-ID) based on the destination address
 2. the enocean library maintains this mapping, which implies the need to store, read, reset,... this
Closed via https://github.com/kipe/enocean/pull/65

I think it would be more intuitive for users if `Packet.data` would correspond to the EnOcean data entries (i.e. DB0..DB3 for 4BS). I know that this would be a major change in the interface, so consider this just as some thoughts.

Without having dived into this problem very deep, i have a guess: EEP F6-02-02 defines two enums 'R1' and 'R2' which have 3 Bits reserved for each of them, but only have defines for value 0...3. So if in the bitstream these 3 Bits have a higher value then 3. the program does not know what to do with this.

> I am wondering if there is a tag in the minimap2 output sam file that indicates whether a read is mapped to the reverse strand of the genome?

Strand is encoded at the FLAG (2nd) field. If bit 16 is set, the read is on the reverse strand. You need to read some tutorial or use a library.

> When a read is mapped to the forward strand of the genome and the gene is also on the forward strand of the genome, the read is on the same strand as transcripts, so we also got "ts:A:+".

Yes

> I am wondering how we could distinguish these two situations (both have "ts:A:+") from the minimap2 output sam file?

Check the alignment strand of the read (i.e. bit 16 of the FLAG field in SAM).
Thanks a lot for this example.

Minimap2 doesn't work well with short RNA-seq reads. If you want to give it a try anyway, you can mix the `splice` and `sr` presets like:
```sh
-a --splice -G200k -ub -C5 --sr -k21 -w6 -A2 -B8 -O12,32 -E2,1 -r50 -p.5 -N20 -f1000,5000 -n2 -m20 -s40 -g200 -2K50m --heap-sort=yes
```
Note that minimap2 won't work with paired-end RNA-seq reads. This is a hard limitation in its algorithm.

Another strategy is to use two mappers, call variants and then take the intersection. You will lose some sensitivity, but you will get low sensitivity anyway with scRNA-seq.

Hey @kidbrax,

Welcome to python and will! 

What you're running into is (unfortunately) common at this point in python's journey.  `3.x` isn't completely backwards compatible with `2.x`, and a number of libraries don't support 3.x yet.

The `0.1.2` version of `wsgiref` is one of them.

There are a few fixes that will get you running, in order of least work to most:

1. Switch over to python2.7 for your will installs.
2. Try to upgrade `wsgiref`  with `pip install --upgrade wsgiref`, and see if the latest version works with your setup, and with will (if it doesn't, you'd notice the http/webhooks stuff not working.
3. If you try 2) and it works, submit a PR here with the upgraded version in requirements.txt.   (You can find out what versions you've got by using `pip freeze`).  

More broadly, it's probably time for me to upgrade *all* the requirements, and refreeze them I've set up #149 for tracking on that work.

Thanks for reaching out on this, and I hope that helps you in getting your will running!

And I promise, python isn't generally like this.  Otherwise, I would have left it years ago. :)

-Steven

Hi @tmarlok88  and @etingof ,
the problem is that  the LDAP protocol uses the BER encoding except for the Boolean value that must be 00 for False and FF for True. So I prefer to not globally switch to CER or DER encodings, that are more restrictive than BER, having only one possible option for a value, because this could work when sending data but not when receiving. The old monkeypatch was needed to restrict the boolean value to FF only when sending, while it allowed any non 0 value when receiving.

The example programs are supposed to be tutorials that teach how to use specific aspects of dlib.  This means they should essentially be essays that happen to be executable programs.  So a bunch of uncommented code doesn't fit that description, even if it does something cool.  A better thing to do is to create a new dlib class that encapsulates the important tool in this program and turn it into a documented and easily used tool.  Then you can use that tool in a short tutorial program that illustrates its use.  Also, please read the how to contribute guidelines for more detailed instructions:  http://dlib.net/howto_contribute.html
http://dlib.net/faq.html#WhywontthePythonbindingscompilework

In the [`0.7.0` release][1] I transposed the data structure (since accessing the points as columns should be contiguous in memory). Sorry for this inconvenience.

So you want:

```python
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: import bezier

In [3]: nodes = np.asfortranarray([
   ...:     [0.0, 1.5, 3.0],
   ...:     [0.0, 1.5, 3.0],
   ...: ])

In [4]: curve = bezier.Curve(nodes, degree=2)

In [5]: ax = curve.plot(100)

In [6]: ax.figure.show()
```

which will produce something like

![figure_1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/520669/37789414-0dd9dbe6-2dc1-11e8-8802-d16ddbd0e2af.png)

----

If you have lots of code already, a "simple" fix is just to make a C-ordered array, and then take the transpose (which well then be Fortran-ordered), e.g.

```python
nodes = np.array([
    [0.0, 0.0],
    [1.5, 1.5],
    [3.0, 0.0],
], order='C').T
```

----

I am going to preemptively close this issue since it seems I have resolved your question.

Hey, it looks like this was from qhull crashing on duplicate 2D points in certain cases, specifically this case:

```    
array([[    0.     ,  -152.4    ],
       [    0.     ,  -152.4    ],
       [-4572.     ,   152.4    ],
       [-4572.     ,   152.4    ],
       [-4572.     ,  -152.4    ],
       [-4572.     ,  -152.4    ],
       [ -185.32131,   152.4    ],
       [ -185.32131,   152.4    ]])
```        
 Previously oriented_bounds_2D (which is ran on every facet of a 3D hull for the 3D OBB) did this:
    `c = ConvexHull(np.asanyarray(points))`

Removing duplicate points before hulling resolves the case you sent over:
```
points = np.asanyarray(points)
points_unique = points[grouping.unique_rows(points)[0]]
c = ConvexHull(points_unique)
```
 
The time hit is noticeable, but worth it given how hard qhull was crashing. Timing both the new and old behavior on a large set of meshes:

With pre merging before 2D hull (new behavior):
```   
In [1]: tic[-1] - tic[0]
Out[1]: 166.44400000572205

 In [2]: np.mean(np.diff(tic))
Out[2]: 0.5642169491719391
```

Old behaviour:
```
In [1]: tic[-1] - tic[0]
Out[1]: 147.22000002861023

In [2]: np.mean(np.diff(tic))
Out[2]: 0.49905084755461093
```

I was thinking about this section:

> Consider a class Employee with a subclass Manager. Now suppose we have a function with an argument annotated with List[Employee]. Should we be allowed to call this function with a variable of type List[Manager] as its argument? Many people would answer "yes, of course" without even considering the consequences. But unless we know more about the function, a type checker should reject such a call: the function might append an Employee instance to the list, which would violate the variable's type in the caller.

> It turns out such an argument acts contravariantly, whereas the intuitive answer (which is correct in case the function doesn't mutate its argument!) requires the argument to act covariantly.

So if function does not mutate the argument, argument can act covariantly.

But you are right that in [483](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0483/#covariance-and-contravariance) they discuss how one should create an immutable container and how:

> Note, that although the variance is defined via type variables, it is not a property of type variables, but a property of generic types.

Just a small comment - applying various geometrical operations to arbitrary complex layouts is a challenging task. Doing simple things on simple layouts (like sizing a rectangle) is simple, but doing this on geometries containing very small angles, or acute angles, or holes, etc. is non-trivial. The best software for this sort of things, in my opinion, is Calibre (from Mentor Graphics - now part of Siemens).

I tried to do resizing (you call it offsetting) of your example in another tool (klayout) - it avoided the problems that gdspy had (apparently, algorithms bugs), but had some other issues (narrow inward triangular cuts caused by two neighboring edges that are almost parallel).

In one of my previous companies, we had out own computational geometry library, and we had to continuously improve it until all issues were resolved (and new issues were showing up for some new unusual geometries in GDS files).

Absolutely, we're not talking about changing the library to do anything except add vanilla support for TCP.  We completely understand your concerns and agree 100% that support for TCP should not in any case involve changes to the way the library is used or add significant complexity.

The point of my original post was just to explain our use-case for TCP, where the code *using* the library needs to be able to confirm whether the metric was received by statsd.

Some more background might make things clearer.  We have various systems that produce metrics and send them to local collectors via UDP.  Those metrics are then passed to a rabbitmq exchange, from which they are consumed by our worker process which sends them to statsd (hopefully using pystatsd) for ingestion into graphite, as well as by other consumers.  If statsd isn't available we would like to have pystatsd let us know that the transmission failed so we can NACK the message back to rabbitmq and it can be retried, something we can't do via UDP.

Currently there is no way to use `pass_context` with function definitions for use in expression. The idea was to make all the data available in the low-level graph and project this data using expressions into high-level graph. So every IO/side-effects are performed by low-level graph, and expressions are just pure functions without side-effects – as explicit as possible.

And you actually can use context in the function definitions, but explicitly, and this can be done by exposing data from the context in the low-level graph (context is also a source of data):
```python
LOW_LEVEL = Graph([
    Root([
        Field('context', None, expose_context),
    ]),
])

HIGH_LEVEL = Graph([
    Node('foo', [
        Field('bar', None, foo_subgraph.c(
            some_func(S.context, S.this.bar)
                      ^^^^^^^^^
        )),
    ]),
])
```
And I'd suggest not to expose the whole context as one field, but expose only what needs to be exposed as distinct fields.

I didn't make any substantive changes to `interpolate_intervals` (all previous tests still pass).  The function itself is strange and a little broken IMO, but this isn't the right place to fix it.

The logic is: for each time point `t`, find the first interval `k` that starts after `t`, go back to the previous segment `k-1`, and apply that segment's label to `t`.  This obviously requires ordered intervals to work, ie, `interval[k-1]` immediately precedes `interval[k]`.

The intervals in your example should not valid. They should be of the form:

```
>>> intervals = np.array([[1., 2.], [2., 3.]])
>>> np.concatenate((intervals.flatten()[::2], [intervals[-1, -1]]), axis=0)
array([ 1.,  2.,  3.])
```

Azure has Key Vault, which is like HSM as a service. It has a [RESTful API](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/keyvault/index), and provides primitives for creating keys and signing blobs with those keys, among others.

Signing data looks like this:

```python
# access_token was procured via standard API call
headers = { 'Authorization': 'Bearer {}'.format(access_token) }

# API payload
data = { 'alg': 'RS256', 'value': some_base64_blob }

# API call, 1 is the key version
requests.post('https://my-key-vault.vault.azure.net/keys/my-key/1/sign?api-version=2016-10-01', json=data, headers=headers)
```

So it seems to me that the intuition for the abstraction expressed above in `make_sig()` is correct. I also see a similar logic exists, albeit internally, in [ocspbuilder/__init__.py](https://github.com/wbond/ocspbuilder/blob/64e7a1f02615e8ec1e7826a097883c480ff73a1c/ocspbuilder/__init__.py#L1028):

```python
if responder_private_key.algorithm == 'rsa':
    sign_func = asymmetric.rsa_pkcs1v15_sign
elif responder_private_key.algorithm == 'dsa':
    sign_func = asymmetric.dsa_sign
elif responder_private_key.algorithm == 'ec':
    sign_func = asymmetric.ecdsa_sign

if not is_oscrypto:
    responder_private_key = asymmetric.load_private_key(responder_private_key)
signature_bytes = sign_func(responder_private_key, response_data.dump(), self._hash_algo)
```

It also makes sense to me that this is a property of the key rather than just a hook. When working with an HSM, it's possible that the entire flow changes a bit, so not just *sign*, but even the initial *load key* primitive is different/nonexistent.

To illustrate a backwards-incompatible change that offers significant performance gain, consider this simple microbenchmark:
```python
from timeit import timeit
from kafka.protocol.message import Message

SHORT_KEY = b"KEY"
SHORT_VALUE = b"VALUE"
LONG_KEY = SHORT_KEY * 1000
LONG_VALUE = SHORT_VALUE * 1000
TS = 1505171828210

def create(size):
    if size == "short":
        key = SHORT_KEY
        value = SHORT_VALUE
    elif size == "long":
        key = LONG_KEY
        value = LONG_VALUE
    return Message(value, key, magic=1, timestamp=TS)

def encode(size):
    m = short_created if size == "short" else long_created
    return m.encode()

def decode(size):
    encoded = short_encoded if size == "short" else long_encoded
    return Message.decode(encoded)

short_created = create("short")
long_created = create("long")
short_encoded = encode("short")
long_encoded = encode("long")

count = 1000000
tmpl = "%-25s%-8.2f%.0f"
print("%-25s%-8s%s" % ("Test", "Time", "Msg/s"))
for size in ("short", "long"):
    setup = "from __main__ import create, encode, decode, size"
    create_time = timeit("create(size)", setup=setup, number=count)
    print(tmpl % ("Create %s" % size, create_time, count / create_time))
    encode_time = timeit("encode(size)", setup=setup, number=count)
    print(tmpl % ("Encode %s" % size, encode_time, count / encode_time))
    decode_time = timeit("decode(size)", setup=setup, number=count)
    print(tmpl % ("Decode %s" % size, decode_time, count / decode_time))
```

The baseline on my development host looks like this:
```
$ python bench.py
Test                     Time    Msg/s
Create short             3.35    298683
Encode short             8.37    119542
Decode short             11.59   86265
Create long              3.50    285579
Encode long              11.68   85591
Decode long              13.88   72036
```

Simply removing the WeakMethod assignment within Message.__init__ and calling Message._encode_self() directly leads to a significant performance gain during Message instantiation (so both Creation and Decoding) and a modest but noticeable performance gain during encoding:
```
$ git diff
diff --git a/bench.py b/bench.py
index 35edcb4..f2dd131 100755
--- a/bench.py
+++ b/bench.py
@@ -21,7 +21,7 @@ def create(size):

 def encode(size):
     m = short_created if size == "short" else long_created

@lgrangeia please pardon me for the long delay. I was not aware of `s_update` before.

The String class (`boofuzz/boofuzz/primitives/string.py`) does not have a `value` property but rather a private `_value` property. This was part of me trying to incorporate the programming practice of encapsulation. Apparently I missed this case.

That said, `s_update` is rather and unusual and it could end up going away.

Given your description of wanting to fuzz once and then fuzz again, I think we can find another approach. Have you considered creating different messages? The code for this would look something like:

    s_initialize("request")
    s_string(name="stuff", value="value")

    s_initialize("newrequest")
    s_update(name="newstuff", value="newvalue")

And when connecting to your `Session`:

    session.connect(s_get("request"))
    session.connect(s_get("newrequest"))

This adds two nodes off of root -- two independent protocol messages. See http://boofuzz.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user/quickstart.html for more context for this kind of example.

Let me know if this seems like a good fit for your use case. If not, maybe we should fix `s_update`, or look into other options.

In case you're wondering, this seems weird to me because the protocol definition itself should be static, and in the ideal case a close mapping to the actual protocol. Using different default values makes me wonder if this is essentially a different kind of message in the same protocol.

@ski7777 what device are you planning to use?

Raspberry Pi's with the 40-pin headers have 2x I2C buses: 
* port 1 on pins BCM_2 & BCM_3, and 
* port 0 on BCM_0 and BCM_1

You should be able to create 2 devices as follows (untested, but _should_ work):

```python

device0 = ssd1306(i2c(port=0, address=0x3C))
device1 = ssd1306(i2c(port=1, address=0x3C))

with canvas(device0) as draw:
    text.draw((0,0), text="BCM_0 and BCM_1", fill="white")

with canvas(device1) as draw:
    text.draw((0,0), text="BCM_2 and BCM_2", fill="white")
```

This prevents an issue with `None`. `None` is a value. I passes in `None` and it prints bogus `{empty_placeholder}` instead of `None` on placeholders that can have `None` or happens to get `None` sometimes... due to this:
```python
value = value_ = get_params(self.key)
```

This also avoids an issue where we want to apply `:2f`, `:g`, etc to all placeholders... and the placeholders themselves are sometimes given `None` for actual values, eg `lm_sensors`.
```python3
        placeholders = self.py3.get_placeholders_list(self.format_sensor)
        format_sensor = {x: ':g' for x in placeholders if x != 'name'}
        self.sensor_placeholders = [x for x in placeholders if x != 'name']
        self.format_sensor = self.py3.update_placeholder_formats(
            self.format_sensor, format_sensor
        )
```
This may also means a slightly new behavior where if users made mistakes in their `format`, we stop making a big fuss out of it in the `format` by printing bogus `{empty_placeholder}` and instead will print all values as-is (ie all non-formatted values that actually exists).

Here's a quick demonstration:

Set up to get the to the point where we have the 5 numpy arrays and two expression:

```python
import opt_einsum as oe
import numpy as np
import dask.array as da
from dask import delayed

sizes = {l: 10 for l in 'abcde'}

contraction1 = 'ab,dca,eb,cde'
terms1 = contraction1.split(',')

contraction2 = 'ab,cda,eb,cde'
terms2 = contraction2.split(',')

# define the intial set of arrays
inputs = sorted(set((*terms1, *terms2)))
np_arrays = {s: np.random.randn(*(sizes[c] for c in s)) for s in inputs}
```

Now convert them to ``dask`` arrays and perform the contraction:

```python
da_arrays = {s: da.from_array(np_arrays[s], chunks=1000) for s in inputs}

# select dask arrays for each expression
ops1  = [da_arrays[s] for s in terms1]
ops2  = [da_arrays[s] for s in terms2]

# contract!
dy1 = oe.contract(contraction1, *ops1, backend='dask')
dy2 = oe.contract(contraction2, *ops2, backend='dask')

# just wrap them in delayed so as to combine in the same computation
dy3 = delayed([dy1, dy2])
dy3.compute()
[77.13078823390276, -41.30794090122288]
```

And we can check that the intermediaries are being used by visualizing the task graph:

```python
dy3.visualize(optimize_graph=True)
```

As you state correctly, if there was no variability in returns (the 2.5% flat every year), sharpe ratio would approach infinity (bad, but still not so bad because, at the same time, our idea of having a ‘risk adjusted’ return measure would be rendered useless, since there would simply be nothing in the equation being ever close to ‘risky’).
Sharpe Ratio measures the ‘risk adjusted excess return’, i.e., how much we make (in excess of the risk free rate) per one unit of risk we take. No risk, no measure.

I think I got the code (thanks to your bitesize chunks), so here are my two cents:

* Averaging returns *
If you have ‘typical’ returns, measured over a discrete period, and you want to average/cumulate them over time you would need to use the geometric mean in order to arrive at the appropriate average (i.e. . (1+r_avg) = (1+i_1)*(1+i_2)…), nasty stuff..). Simple averaging will not do (winning +50% and then loosing -50% will give you a total performance of -25%, not 0%).
To make life easier, use continuously compounded (or log) returns! This has a number of advantages:
One assumption underlying the sharpe ratio as a risk adjusted measure is normality of returns (although it would at least be defined for any return distribution). Log returns are closer to normally distributed and hence better suited. 
Normality is further crucial for being able to properly scale returns/std dev in time (we do it although it comes with a price tag attached, see the often cited paper by Lo (2002) The statistics of sharpe ratios, http://edge-fund.com/Lo02.pdf. Just never stop questioning ourselves).
And finally, who would have thought, those log returns have a nice property: cumulating or averaging returns over time simplifies to adding or arithmetically averaging individual returns. 

* Estimating standard deviation  *
from only two annual values like it is done in this example has two hefty caveats:
Firstly, results will be statistically extremely weak, ideally you should use 30 or more data points. Secondly, by compressing the time frame so radically before the actual calculation, you lose all the inter-year information about the actual movements of the asset, basically wiping out any of the up-and-then-down-moves (which very exactly leaves you with such small values for sd). Why throw all this information away?
Better calculate std dev from monthly or daily returns and then annualize it. If they are normally distributed, we can do so by multiplying with the square root of the time scaling factor (there are 252 trading days in one year: -> ann. Std. dev. =  daily std dev. * sqrt(252) ) 

* Estimating standard deviation 2 *
a small thing indeed but still there: while estimating the standard deviation you should not divide by N but by N-1 (why not use built-in functions anyway?)

I am unclear how you arrive at the annual returns you state from the quick start cross-over example (since mine, calculated manually, being 14.89% (2005) and 5.05% (2006) but I might have made mistakes in the SMA cross-over).
For a manual calculation, for the sake of simplicity, let’s just consider a simple buy and hold strategy (i.e., we look at the asset itself)

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

data = pd.read_csv('Z:/.../backtrader/datas/2005-2006-day-001.txt', index_col=0, parse_dates=True)

#risk free rate, converted to log return
rf = 0.01
rf_log = np.log(1 + rf)

# daily discrete return
ret_d = data.Close / data.Close.shift(1) - 1

# transform to daily log returns
logret_d = np.log(1 + ret_d)

# to get the total return of the strategy, just add them (yeah, we know, no problem with log rets) 
logret_sum = logret_d.cumsum()

# last value: total cumulated return over the entire period
logret_total = logret_sum[-1] 

# scale it to annual (assuming 252 trading days a year) 
logret_annual = logret_total * np.sqrt(252 / len(logret_sum))

# standard deviation of daily log returns ...
stddev_d = logret_d.std() 

# ... scaled to annual
stddev_a = stddev_d * np.sqrt(252)

# sharpe ratio
SR = (logret_annual - rf_log) / stddev_a


SR here comes out with 1.7068 which, while looking at the chart, seems quite reasonable.

This sharp ratio we just calculated is on an annual basis. Since we can scale returns and std dev in time, why should we not be able to do the same with the entire sharpe ratio? With which factor? Well, since we used N for (log!!) returns and sqrt(N) for sd, it must be sqrt(N) (since N divded by sqrt(N) is again sqrt(N)).


This is also a nice piece I just stumbled across, on general issues with the SR:
https://www.crystalbull.com/sharpe-ratio-better-with-log-returns/

One other suggestion you could consider: in using the sharpe ratio we accept the standard deviation as a measure of the risk we exposure ourselves to. This means that we not only punish large downward movements, but also large moves upward. This is somewhat counter intuitive since we actually like up-moves (very much actually, much more than downward movements). For the SR both is equally bad. Therefore, an interesting measure which deals with this flaw could be the Sortino ratio which is basically equal to the SR, just any upward returns are set to zero before calculation, basically removing any positive jumps from the std dev calculation.

Hi Michael! Really glad you're using flask-table, hope I can help you. Using Python's formatting and the `>` and `<` operators adds whitespace to a string, which will the be ignored when the browser renders the HTML which is why that isn't working. To tackle the alignment you would need to turn to CSS instead, and you can add classes to the columns in your table using the `column_html_attrs` keyword (and `th_html_attrs` and `td_html_attrs`). For example (you should be able to run this file and experiment): ```python from flask_table import Table, Col from flask import Flask, render_template_string app = Flask(__name__) class ItemTable(Table): colA = Col('ColA', column_html_attrs={'class': 'colA'}) # apply to both and s colB = Col('ColB', th_html_attrs={'class': 'colB'}) # apply just to the colC = Col('ColC', td_html_attrs={'class': 'colC'}) # apply just to the s DEMO_TEMPLATE_STRING = ''' {{ table }} ''' @app.route('/') def index(): items = [ dict(colA='1', colB='long-thing-here1', colC='1'), dict(colA='2', colB='long-thing-here2', colC='2'), ] table = ItemTable(items) return render_template_string(DEMO_TEMPLATE_STRING, table=table) if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(debug=True) ``` Would generate HTML (for the table) like: ```html
ColA ColB ColC
1 long-thing-here1 1
2 long-thing-here2 2
``` with the CSS adjusting the alignment for the elements with the classes. There's another example about using these keyword arguments to put attributes on the ``s and the ``s here: https://github.com/plumdog/flask_table/blob/master/examples/column_html_attrs.py Hope some of that is helpful, let me know if you have any other questions.

Though "attr" is now getting a bit overloaded (`attr` and `attr_list` to mean "this is how to get a thing from the python object for this row" and `column_attrs`, `th_attrs`, `td_attrs` to mean "put these key-values into the html element"). Which isn't totally lovely, but "attribute" is the right name in both cases, and I don't think the names are horribly confusing. PR #209 should fix this issue.

When we resolve identities internally, we store the `prefix`-prefixed version and the plain string, but not the module name prefixed. This meant that where an identityref ended up being prefixed (only required when the `identity` is defined outside of the current module), then we didn't have the right lookup key to figure it was a valid value.

PR re-works this by stripping the prefix if it's encountered. PTAL.

For one, that breaks duck typing. You have to try and catch then retry a different open. This is a big, known problem, but last I heard people were saying it would be 'to hard' to find and fix every open function in Python's stdlib. I haven't seen much development on pathlib since it was introduced.

Support would have to be added to more than open, too; any open function from the stdlib or 3rd party libraries like numpy and pandas would have to be patched too. A standard way to do it would make sense; the current way open works is it checks isinstance(str) and then tries a buffer interface if it doesn't work. I would rather see a special property `__filename__`  added to `str` and `pathlib` and any 3rd party library that wants to do paths, or have the open sequence change to try buffer first, then basically `open(str(...))` if that fails. Or, maybe make an abc that all paths should register for, including pathlib...

Yes, the list will unpack the value from the passed node and put it into a new `dllistnode` or `sllistnode`.

Unfortunately we can't just insert the node from the argument because it may already belong to another list and nodes can have only one owner.
Changing this behavior could break existing code. For example, in the following snippet we would have to throw an exception in the last line to prevent sharing the inserted node between two lists.
```
from llist import dllist

a = dllist([1234])
b = dllist()
b.append(a.first)
```
Currently the code successfully appends the value to `b`, by wrapping it in a new node.

`grammar.parse` uses by default the top rule, which means that you are trying to parse `'i am bad'` with the `sent_good` rule. Which is why it will not work.

So you could add a new rule on top of the grammar with the following body: `sent_good / sent_bad`. At the [syntax reference](https://github.com/erikrose/parsimonious#syntax-reference), this is called alternation.

An other way to do the same would be to do `i _ am _ (good / bad)`, but I think that boils down to preference, and what you ultimately want to build with this.

Alternatively, you can lookup a rule and try to parse with it like this: `grammar['sent_bad'].parse('i am bad')`, but that does require you to know a bit in advance, which might defeat the purpose of parsing.

I hope it helps :)

I found a blocker to add support for v3 of the library. With the refactoring done to the library, one of the call we use in our tool: `save`, is very slow with v3. The issue is that the stream is now read byte by byte since the [chunk size is set to 1](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/3.0.1-release/docker/api/client.py#L356). A simple busybox image is exported in 10 seconds where it should be done in zero time as it was before. Imagine if you would squash a 2BG image...

I've opened this ticket: https://github.com/docker/docker-py/issues/1905 See also linked issue for similar reports.

My proposal for now is to update requirement to use version < 3.0.0 of the library and leave this open until the upstream client will be fixed.

Hi @jayspeidell 

Thank you for your contribution and welcome to PySwarms! :+1: I noticed that you are working with both the single-objective functions and the plotters library in the same Pull Request. I'd recommend to:
1. Keep *only* the single-objective-related changes in this PR, and 
2. Open up an Issue + new PR for the changes in the `plotters` library.

We want to keep all changes as atomic as possible, so we can have a concise history of the project :+1: . I know it may be a bit of a hassle for you but if you need any help, we're always available!

So this is what should happen:
```
development (branch) -------
                           \---------- jayspeidell:add-functions
                           \---------- jayspeidell:fix-plotters-module          
```
Both branches will be merged into the development branch after review :+1: 

## In your local machine
In Git, what you can do is:
1. Make sure you have a local copy of all your changes as backup
2.  [`revert`](https://www.atlassian.com/git/tutorials/undoing-changes/git-revert) all commits related to the plotters module, **or** do [`git rebase`](https://www.atlassian.com/git/tutorials/rewriting-history#git-rebase-i) and delete them.
3. Push changes to `jayspeidell:add-functions`, making sure that only changes in the single-objective functions (or specifically, `single_obj.py`) were done.
4. Open up a new Issue, describing the fix and changes needed for the `plotters` module. (I know this is a hassle, but we really need a well-documented history of the project :+1: )
5. Make a new branch in your own fork,  `jayspeidell:fix-plotters-module`
6. Copy or do all changes only related to the `plotters` module
7. Make a new Pull Request! :100: 

And that's it! 

Thank you for your help and we really appreciate your contribution to PySwarms! Take your time and feel free to ask us if you have any questions!

It can be done fairy easy using `loop.create_task`. Didn't run code below
```
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import asyncio
import asynqp


USERS = {'betty', 'bob', 'luis', 'tony'}
TASKS = set([])

def process_msg(msg):
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    task = loop.create_task(process_msg_coro(msg))
    task.add_done_callback(lambda t: TASKS.remove(t))
    TASKS.add(task)
    
async def process_msg_coro(msg):
    print('>> {}'.format(msg.body))
    # You can now do await's here
    msg.ack()

async def connect():
    connection = await asynqp.connect(host='dev_queue', virtual_host='asynqp_test')
    channel = await connection.open_channel()
    exchange = await channel.declare_exchange('inboxes', 'direct')

    # we have 10 users. Set up a queue for each of them
    # use different channels to avoid any interference
    # during message consumption, just in case.
    for username in USERS:
        user_channel = await connection.open_channel()
        queue = await user_channel.declare_queue('Inbox_{}'.format(username))
        await queue.bind(exchange, routing_key=username)
        await queue.consume(process_msg)

    # deliver 10 messages to each user
    for username in USERS:
        for msg_idx in range(10):
            msg = asynqp.Message('Msg #{} for {}'.format(msg_idx, username))
            exchange.publish(msg, routing_key=username)


loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(connect())
try:
    loop.run_forever()
finally:
    loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.wait(TASKS))
```

* You would want add a finalization to all of your components, as closing the loop without it might get you with broken connections and undefined state. In this case we wait for pending tasks to be completed before closing the application.

Translated to this context this could look something liket this:

```
def something(self):
    # ...
    warnings.warn("Check.script should take a list of args", DeprecationWarning)
    return {'args': ["sh", "-c", script], 'interval': interval}
```

This way you can emit the deprecation warning but you don't break the user.

        self.format_finished = format_finished
        self.format = format

(Obligatory preface: I am not a lawyer, and none of my comments based on reading some FAQs on the internet constitute legal advice.) This is a pretty complex question. Per [this FAQ entry](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-faq.html#LinkingWithGPL):
> **You have a GPL'ed program that I'd like to link with my code to build a proprietary program. Does the fact that I link with your program mean I have to GPL my program?**
> Not exactly. It means you must release your program under a license compatible with the GPL (more precisely, compatible with one or more GPL versions accepted by all the rest of the code in the combination that you link). The combination itself is then available under those GPL versions.

My naive interpretation of that answer is that the **combined** work (i.e. a binary or source distribution of the library **and** its dependencies) must be licensed under the GPL, but the library itself need only be licensed under a GPL compatible license ([see also](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-faq.html#IfLibraryIsGPL)). Because you're depending on a library but not actually distributing a combined work, you likely only need to use one of the compatible licenses rather than the GPLv3 itself. Among those compatible licenses are (I'm assuming these cover the options being considered under "MIT or BSD" above):

- [3-clause BSD](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/license-list.en.html#ModifiedBSD)
- [2-clause BSD](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/license-list.en.html#FreeBSD)
- [Expat](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/license-list.en.html#Expat) ("It is sometimes ambiguously referred to as the MIT License.")
- [X11](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/license-list.en.html#X11License) ("This license is sometimes called the MIT license")

Based on those links, it seems like as long as you pick a GPL compatible license for the library, it should be compliant with the terms of the dependency's license.

**TL;DR**: you're probably fine choosing one of those licenses for this library.

This will kinda work, but you've got a latent problem here. If, at a later
point, a developer puts in the RevisionMiddleware, or otherwise wraps your
function in another create_revision() call, then your signal handler will
disconnect before the revision is created. That's the crux of the problem.

Provided nobody else does this, I think this will work. To be sane, it
might be worth protecting the post_revision_commit signal with a
threading.Lock().

As pvizeli said, the problem lies in the way the XML-RPC works. We don't know in advance which keys we can use to ask for values, so we have to try each one that's available. Performing the request with a key that is not "active" in your setup leads to the exception. But to know it is not active we have to do the request. So, yeah, that's a typical "Henne-Ei"-Problem.
If I understood correctly, this only happens while HASS is starting. Afterwards the values get updated through events, and I assume the CCU won't send events for inactive keys. So it's fully functional, and there are only two ERROR-message in the logs. Am I correct? If so, I would close this issue and mark it _wontfix_.

One question that I've been thinking about with a distinct relevance to LSTM is how many times to feed a set of inputs to a non-continuous recurrent network before reading the outputs. For a feedforward network, the input goes through the entire set of layers before the output is read. I'm thinking this might be something for a genome attribute (an IntAttribute as with @bennr01's hyperneat branch) to determine. (I also need to try to look up any prior research on this... haven't quite gotten around to it, as I am not focusing on recurrent networks at the moment.)

Re rawal.gecco2016.pdf - Amusing; I just referenced that paper over on another (#105) issue discussion.

Setting up a static LSTM configuration would be one way to do it; otherwise, it would need to be a type of node with multiple distinct inputs - something that will be needed anyway for RBF nodes. One could probably reduce the number of inputs to A. data and B. forget old vs ignore new - as in, the second input would be a combined forget+write trigger (ordering of wipe and load new from "data" would need to be correct, of course!). I don't see any need for an output gate, which seems more needed for more-static networks using LSTMs.

> How are you preventing duplicates with the randomization?

The philosophy of the UUID type is that you're using so many bits (128) that the probability of a collision is astronomically low. See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universally_unique_identifier#Collisions for the math.

> what's the advantage?

The advantage is that you can discard your indexer at any time and make a new one when you need it. I needed this behavior in https://github.com/mathiasose/CA-NEAT to parallelize the algorithm using `celery`. In my custom `celery` EA loop there was no process that lived for the entire lifetime of the experiment, so nowhere to keep the counter. I thought about passing the counter value as a message from one process to another, but went with the random indices since I thought it was simpler.

About the CA-NEAT repo: the code is a bit messy (I didn't have a set plan, just implemented things as I needed them) and not suitable for merging into neat-python directly, so I'm re-implementing some of my changes that I think might be useful to others.

Thanks for the detailed bug report. You're right, the behaviour is not optimal and I will improve it. I will fix it in wsgi-request-logger but you can work-around this issue by setting the "Content-Length" header in your application. If you [call `send_file()` with a file name instead of a file-like object, this is done automatically](http://stackoverflow.com/a/25221517/183995). If you really need to provide `send_file()` with a file-like object, you can also set the content_length yourself (and you should, if there's any way of knowing it the moment you create the stream to let Browsers display an e.t.a.). In your example, this could be done like this:

````python
@app.route('/file', methods=['GET'])
def serve_file():
    f = open('data', 'rb')
    sf = send_file(f, mimetype="application/ocet-stream")
    sf.headers['Content-Length'] = str(os.path.getsize('data'))
    return sf
````
Concerning the how-to-fix-it in wsgi-request-logger I'm thinking about something like this:

````python
        if content_lengths :
            content_length = content_lengths[0]
        elif all(hasattr(retval, attr) for attr in ('seek', 'tell')):
            retval.seek(0, 2)
            content_length = retval.tell()
            retval.seek(0)
        elif hasattr(retval, 'file') and hasattr(retval.file, 'seek') and hasattr(retval.file, 'tell'):
            retval.file.seek(0, 2)
            content_length = retval.file.tell()
            retval.file.seek(0)
        else:
            content_length = len(b''.join(retval))
````

Not optimal yet... If you're really streaming content where you don't know the content size at time when you start serving it (e.g. live content such as mjpeg frames from a web cam), the `seek(0,2)` will most probably block. So what should the log-file contain for the file size in those cases? I'm not sure.

At a glance it looks like a good start, but I'm sure there is more that would need to go in there. I haven't used flask-jwt in quite a while, so this probably isn't comprehensive, but some other things I can think of right off hand are:

* Doesn't require a function to load users from a token. We have the option to do that, but I'm sure the way we set that up is different than in flask-jwt
* You have to create your own auth endpoint that generates tokens (with create_access_token() and create_refresh_token() methods), instead of letting flask-jwt handle this for you. The reason for this is to give the programmer more power and not lock them into one way of doing things.
* Support for all the optional features (blacklisting, refresh tokens, fresh tokens, cookies, csrf, etc).
* I'm guessing there are probably more option names that are different between the two libraries, but don't know right off hand what they would be.
* Different way to add custom data into the JWT claims and change errors that are raised when invalid tokens are encountered.

At it's core, this is a different library then flask-jwt (it isn't a fork of flask-jwt, it was built from the ground up). If you wanted to put together some documentation for migrating from flask-jwt, I would totally welcome a pull request. If you are just curious for yourself, I think looking at these are a good start, but poking through the documentation would be your best bet (and I'm available on IRC to answer any questions you might have).

Cheers! :)

As a temporary solution I use custom Namespace with hardcoded field  _connected = True. 
You need to include this namespace while initializing SocketIO and than also in `socketIO.define()`, because in `_should_stop_waiting` loop method returns **False** and stops iterating when default namespace `'/'` doesn't have `_connected = True`.

So here's my workaround:

```python
class Namespace(LoggingNamespace):
    _connected = True

    def on_connect(self):
        print('connected')

    def on_error(self, data):
        print('error')

with SocketIO('http://0.0.0.0', 8080, Namespace) as socketIO:
    nsp = socketIO.define(Namespace, '/my_namespace')
    nsp.emit('my message', 'hello')
    socketIO.wait(seconds=1)
```

> Now imagine we send 1 million messages, and imagine we use some slow (and probably third-party) storage for the email templates.

Note that this case hasn't been considered in my implementation yet, but it is trivial to fix: Add the ability to pass a loaded template.

```diff
- def email_message(template_name, …):
-    template = get_template(template_name)
+ def email_message(template, …):
+    if isinstance(template, str):
+        template = get_template(template)
```

In my benchmarks, this speeds up mail-templated-simple's performance from 4x slower to about 1.3x slower.

The short answer is 'because that's what the [upstream code](https://github.com/googlei18n/libphonenumber) also [does](http://libphonenumber.appspot.com/phonenumberparser?number=%2B442083661177--)'.   Ask upstream for a definitive answer, but I think it allows for e.g. extensions.

This Python port tracks the upstream libphonenumber Java code, and so I don't generally take changes that diverge from that. 

Also, I'm not sure this is a sensible approach -- holding per-region information on each phone number object seems inefficient & not a great match to the purpose of the `PhoneNumber` object (and there's already a `PhoneMetadata` class that holds per-region information, which includes the 2-letter country code in particular).

> protocol which actually streams the data. that protocol seems to be RTP
> (which from a preliminary look, should be easy to convert to something like
> an MP4 or something else which we can easily use output from). The SIP
> messaging responds with information on how to access this RTP stream, and
> it seems like it's randomized somewhat so we can't just skip the whole SIP
> messaging step and grab the stream.
>
> It also looks like the version of SIP here uses some extensions (the
> second RFC I linked) to "subscribe" to something. That subscription request
> is being denied which is why i'm guessing these streams always end like 30
> seconds after I start them.
>
> I'm fairly sure we can get the SUBSCRIBE stuff to work by just
> impersonating the ring client, which means we can get a full stream
> (including 2-way audio if you want it) working.
>
> All that being said, I'm gonna give this a shot. I haven't worked with
> python for like 7 years, so i'm hoping if I can get a "proof-of-concept"
> working, someone else can take the reins and actually get it working well.
>
> My next step is to get some of this SIP messaging working in python. I'm
> going to use the Twisted library which does seem to have some support for
> SIP (here's the protocol definition in twisted
> <https://github.com/twisted/twisted/blob/twisted-17.1.0/src/twisted/protocols/sip.py>
> ).

Update 2:
For another unexpected behavior, I simplify the example to be following:
```python
def test2():
    a = nd.ones((2, 3), ctx=ctx)  # a is 1
    a.attach_grad()
    d = nd.zeros((2, 3), ctx=ctx) + 100  # d is 100
    d.attach_grad()
    with ag.record():
        b = a * 2
        t = b * d
        t.backward()
        PP(d.grad)
    with ag.record():
        b = a * 2
        t = b * 3
        t.backward()
        PP(d.grad)
```
Both runs give `d.grad` equal to 2. However, in the second graph, `d` is never used so `d.grad` should be zero. I guess here `d` simply keeps the gradient value of the last computation graph. I think this behavior is still ok (and should warn user in doc or something), but should be consistent when `d.grad` is attached using `add` requirement.

I've just run into this issue too after upgrading from v1.4.4 to v1.7.4

This *is* the expected behaviour when using `auto_now_add` or `auto_now` (see [the docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/ref/models/fields/#django.db.models.DateField.auto_now))

It looks to me as though this was introduced in v1.5.6, more specifically, commit 818f602214a3ea8144c184d5cf1ed41de0ae426c

I'd be inclined to call this a bug in this library, since it's an undocumented, backwards-incompatible change.

There are three possible fixes for you:
 1. add some arbitrary values for the `created` and `modified` fields
 2. stop inheriting from `TimeStampedModel` and just add the `created` and `modified` fields directly on your model, and pass `default=timezone.now` to them. This will cause the created and modified values to be (re)set every time you load your fixture, which may or may not be what you want.
 3. remove the fields entirely from your model if they're not required

If you're project is like mine, it might be that someone decided to make every model in the project inherit from the `TimeStampedModel` without thinking about whether that was actually desirable or not. If you don't actually need the `created` and `modifed` values, just dump them.

I'm sorry that you got frustrated with this.  I feel like you turned pretty hostile on us and we'd barely gotten started with helping you understand and resolve your issue.

If you do decide to keep watchman installed, and really don't like putting your configuration in `/etc`, I think we should look again at where you placed the `.watchmanconfig` file.  From one of your comments it sounded like you had placed that in your home dir.  That isn't what you want in this case because it is just confirming to watchman that the home dir should be a watch point, and we've established that you don't want to pay the cost of watching that.

For example, if you put your source code under `~/src` and are happy with Nuclide and watchman keeping track of everything under there, then I would recommend creating `~/src/.watchmanconfig` and removing the one you placed in `~/.watchmanconfig`, then running `watchman watch-del-all`.

Regarding the other filesystem watchers that you compared watchman against: watchman was designed to to be fast to query in very large filesystem trees.  Watchman intentionally uses RAM to ensure that the responses are fast and are not constrained by the lackluster performance of the VFS layer in macOS.  The persistent monitoring is an important component of this because it takes too long to rebuild that in-memory database of information from scratch with the size of directory tree that we work with.  The persistent model makes watchman extremely reliable when compared to in-process subscription based watchers that are common in the nodejs ecosystem.

It is understandable that these differences took you by surprise.

The default configuration in watchman has been "good enough" for the majority of our users so far.  We do recognize that it would be good to allow more control over this sort of thing, and have been tracking some brief thoughts on this in https://github.com/facebook/watchman/issues/202.  It just hasn't come up often up for us to raise the priority on this so far.

Could not understand why recv() was hanging in Linux but not Windows in my multiprocessing code. Switching Context.instance() to Context() did the job. Using Python 2.7.14 and 16.0.4

Presumably this is due to the different natures of python multiprocessing on different platforms, forking (linux) vs spawning (win).  My existing logging objects worked without any extra hassle in Linux but are alive yet inert in Windows, which makes some sense because Linux does allow some shared resource space while Windows does not.

We have not, to the best of my knowledge, tested them behind a proxy.

The code use to download the copora, for example for the TED corpus
```python
...
from tensorflow.contrib.learn.python.learn.datasets import base
...

    # Conditionally download data
    TED_DATA = "TEDLIUM_release2.tar.gz"
    TED_DATA_URL = "http://www.openslr.org/resources/19/TEDLIUM_release2.tar.gz"
    local_file = base.maybe_download(TED_DATA, data_dir, TED_DATA_URL)
    ...
```
uses the `base.maybe_download()` method from Tensorflow[[1](https://github.com/mozilla/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/contrib/learn/python/learn/datasets/base.py#L193)] which in turn uses `urllib` from `six.moves` and `urlretrieve`[[2](https://docs.python.org/2/library/urllib.html#urllib.urlretrieve)].

Supposedly `urllib` can work with proxies that do not require authentication if one sets the `HTTP_PROXY` environment variable[[3](http://kaamka.blogspot.de/2009/06/httpproxy-environment-variable.html)]. So I would try that.

You should be able to do `decode(jsonstr, classes=Thing)` or `classes=(Thing, OtherThing, ...))` and it should recognize your local classes when encoded using jsonpickle.  That should solve any problems related to local classes that can't be found by jsonpickle via module imports, which  may have been related here.

if you want to interact with a dict directly, then you should probably use the `jsonpickle.unpickler.Unpickler` class directly since its `restore()` method expects a dict instead of raw json.  Likewise, `jsonpickle.pickler.Pickler.flatten()` to product dicts from objects.

If you want to "decorate" a dict with the metadata to make it decode as if it's a class then you should be able to safely use this snippet.

```
import jsonpickle
import jsonpickle.tags as tags
import jsonpickle.unpickler as unpickler
import jsonpickle.util as util

class Thing(object):
    pass

dct = {}
dct[tags.OBJECT] = util.importable_name(Thing)
dct['name'] = 'awesome'

obj = unpickler.Unpickler().restore(dct, classes=Thing)
assert isinstance(obj, Thing)
assert obj.name == 'awesome'
```

As usual, if `Thing` is importable (and not a local class in an inaccessible scope as detailed in #161) then you don't need `classes=...`.

Hope that helps.

@Sidhesh-telsiz 

It does do chunking. [Here](https://github.com/boto/s3transfer/blob/develop/s3transfer/manager.py#L99) is the underlying parameter that controls the max number of chunks to ensure you do not run out memory. Essentially what the library does is read from the input stream one chunk (~8MB) at a time and waits to read any more chunks to be delegated to worker threads that do the upload part if the current in memory chunks being uploaded is at its maximum.

For the service resource, you can access your stacks via the service resource's ``stacks`` collection. For example:
```py
import boto3

cf = boto3.resource('cloudformation')
for stack in cf.stacks.all():
    print(stack)
```

To validate a template, unfortunately we have not modeled the template as a resource yet. To validate a template using a service resource, you will have to use the client attached to the service resource. So something like:

@datakurre: Thanks for working on finalizing the PR. I overlooked the `python-requests` examples as we don't include those in our docs.

Looking at that section more closely I wonder about the original use cases you had that led you to implement the AST based handling for the entire JSON payload. Given that the input data is by definition already JSON encoded did you consider passing that through as-is to `requests` and perhaps just pretty-printing it in the generated code?

For example, instead of
```python
requests.post('https://api.com/path', headers={'Content-Type':'application/json'}, json={'foo':'bar'}
```
which requires first parsing `{"foo":"bar"}` into a Python object and then have `requests` serialize it again we could just pass the JSON string as-is with the `data` parameter.
```
requests.post('https://api.com/path', headers={'Content-Type':'application/json'}, data='{"foo":"bar"}'
```
It might make sense to extract the data into a variable to allow pretty printing for better readability, e.g.
```python
payload = """
{
  "foo": "bar",
  "bar": [1, 2, 3]
}
"""
requests.post('https://api.com/path', headers={'Content-Type':'application/json'}, data=payload)
```

There might be some differences how the two libraries differ. Also their supporting libraries, mainly selenium, is totally different things.

If the element is hidden or doesn't contain text the underlying Selenium Webdriver returns empty (Python None if recall correctly). If the element is not found, then error should be raised by the Selenium2Library.

In Selenium2Library if there are multiple elements matches to the selector, then only the first found match is used. Then if the selector is not unique, the wrong element text is returned.

Because the above reasons, the valid selector in SeleniumLibrary may return different results when using Selenium2Library. Therefore it would be good to double check the selector and make sure it is unique.

Also this could be a timing issue. If the page is loaded or content is changed dynamically, before the Get Text is used it could explain why empty is returned. If this is the case, polling that page is fully loaded is needed before the Get Text can be used. For a quick hack, simple sleep would prove is this the reason.

The XML.txt you did attached, it was from the DOM and not from the HTML source. The DOM is the only relevant matter in case of the Selenium Webdriver and now days, there should not be any reason to look the HTML source.

As a last thing, the SeleniumLibrary has, at least one, advantage over the Selenium2Library. It is the support for Flex applications. If your app is using Flex, then it could explain the difference between the results.

I've dug a little more. Looks like it's a specific problem that raises when a post has no translations at all. Since it the translation part is parler's responsibility, the way it's done allows this kind of scenery.

So, even with the correction, the final application would still has data problem and this solution would suppress the real problem.

The parler's documentation recommend some ways to deal with missing translation. The use of `safe_translation_getter` is one of them and it depends on the use of the `default` parameter passed to it - if not passed it's `None`.

Translation fallback:
https://django-parler.readthedocs.io/en/latest/advanced/fallback.html

`safe_translation_getter`:
https://github.com/django-parler/django-parler/blob/6324e5f9468dddf7d23d727d12e9870294775742/parler/models.py#L745

I see a design decision here that can lead to different solutions. In my opinion some of the solutions are:

- the @rafaelgontijo suggestion (or the user of `default` parameter in the same way)
- keep raising an error but a more informative one (e.g. there is no translation related to <...>)
- make it easy to program a custom fall back for that (the `default` parameter can be a callable by the way)

Here's one workaround if all you need are the index name and URL (e.g. using `devpi`).  You can invoke this shell function whenever you want to create a Pipfile that has your custom index name and URL but is otherwise pristine.

```
pipenv_init() {
  # pipenv issue #856: pipenv doesn't respect pip.conf
  # https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/856

  # This function accepts one optional argument: the path to the pipenv
  # executable. If not set or empty, defaults to PATH lookup.
  local PIPENV="${1:-pipenv}"

  if [[ -z "${PIPENV_INDEX_NAME-}" ]] || [[ -z "${PIPENV_INDEX_URL-}" ]]; then
    >&2 echo 'PIPENV_INDEX_{NAME,URL} env vars must be set and not empty'
    return 1
  fi

  # Create fresh Pipfile and virtualenv.
  "${PIPENV}" --rm || true
  rm -f -- Pipfile Pipfile.lock
  "${PIPENV}" install
  rm -- Pipfile.lock

  # Within Pipfile's `[[source]]` section, set `name` to
  # `${PIPENV_INDEX_NAME}` and `url` to `${PIPENV_INDEX_URL}`.
  # These two environment variables may not contain vertical bars,
  # due to the use of vertical bars in the `sed` expression.
  local TEMP_PIPFILE="$(mktemp)"
  < Pipfile \
      sed \
      -e '/^\[\[source\]\]$/,/^\[/ { s|^\(name = \).*|\1"'"${PIPENV_INDEX_NAME}"'"| ; s|^\(url = \).*|\1"'"${PIPENV_INDEX_URL}"'"| ; }' \
    > "${TEMP_PIPFILE}"
  mv -- "${TEMP_PIPFILE}" Pipfile
}
```

Pipenv does not have a vendoring command, and it is unlikely to be added in near future (see #1143). I personally don’t think it fits well in Pipenv either.

Something like this would probably suit your need (modified from https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/1143#issuecomment-352535697):

```
pipenv lock -r > foo.txt
pipenv run pip download -d ./vendor -r foo.txt
pipenv run python -m zipfile -c vendor.zip ./vendor/
```

Thanks @puzrin!

I think the implementation might be wrong. I'm saying "I think" because my knowledge of YAML standard is limited and am asking for the clarification from people who maintain this library and most probably have better understanding of the standard.

Here's my reasoning:
Please check the "Example 2.11. Mapping between Sequences" on the following page: http://yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html

As you can see, the standard allows having arrays for keys.
```yaml
? - Detroit Tigers
  - Chicago cubs
:
  - 2001-07-23
```

This is a YAML file with the key array: `['Detroit Tigers', 'Chicago cubs']` which has value (array) `['2001-07-23']`.

If we were to have a comma in the key array:

```yaml
? - Detroit, Tigers
  - Chicago cubs
:
  - 2001-07-23
```

It should convert it into: `['Detroit, Tigers', 'Chicago cubs']`. However, this package doesn't convert it to this syntax correctly. It converts it into: `'Detroit, Tigers,Chicago cubs'`. There are no quotes between the keys, the whole key is considered to be a key.

This website seems to convert it correctly:
https://www.json2yaml.com/

From YAML:
```yaml
? - Detroit, Tigers
  - Chicago cubs
:
  - 2001-07-23
```

To JSON:
```json
{
  "[\"Detroit, Tigers\", \"Chicago cubs\"]": [
    "2001-07-23"
  ]
}
```

What do you think @puzrin?
I believe this is a faulty behavior.

- You need to figure out what process Windows still has a lock on the temp file; does your software use any subprocesses? Have you made sure those subprocesses have terminated by the time `tempdir` attempts its cleanup?

- What's left over in one of the temp directories that's left behind? There's a big list at the end of the test run, have a look inside one of those and it may give you a clue as to what still has a lock

- Be aware that Windows and its filesystems are particularly crappy in this area, see [this code](https://github.com/Simplistix/testfixtures/blob/master/testfixtures/rmtree.py) that I worked into testfixtures back when I still was doing Windows dev.

- If you do find a problem, a PR to testfixtures that added an appveyor run and tests to highlight the problem would be very gratefully received.

Good luck and happy hunting!

@fdeugenio  Many thanks for this contribution.  Having an exact method for rectangular apertures is a nice addition!

However, let me explain why this change should not go into `photutils`.  The low-level Cython geometry functions (e.g `rectangular_overlap.pyx`) currently in `photutils` are moving upstream to the astropy `regions` package (https://github.com/astropy/regions).  The plan is that `regions` will eventually move to the `astropy` core package (soonish, hopefully).  The `geometry` functions have already been moved to `regions` and the very near term they will be deprecated in `photutils`.  Therefore, this change should go in `regions` instead of `photutils` (I'm sorry that there was no way for you to know this plan).  This is a nice contribution, so please submit this PR to the `regions` package.

```
def segment_lung_mask(image, fill_lung_structures = True):

    # not actually binary, but 1 and 2. 
    # 0 is treated as background, which we do not want
    binary_image = np.array(image > -320, dtype = np.int8)+1
    labels = measure.label(binary_image)

    # Pick the pixel in the very corner to determine which label is air.
    #   Improvement: Pick multiple background labels from around the patient
    #   More resistant to "trays" on which the patient lays cutting the air 
    #   around the person in half
    background_label = labels[0,0,0]

    #Fill the air around the person
    binary_image[background_label == labels] = 2


    # Method of filling the lung structures (that is superior to something like 
    # morphological closing)
    if fill_lung_structures:
        # For every slice we determine the largest solid structure
        for i, axial_slice in enumerate(binary_image):
            axial_slice = axial_slice - 1
            labeling = measure.label(axial_slice)
            l_max = largest_label_volume(labeling, bg=0)

            if l_max is not None: #This slice contains some lung
                binary_image[i][labeling != l_max] = 1

    binary_image -= 1 #Make the image actual binary
    binary_image = 1-binary_image # Invert it, lungs are now 1

    # Remove other air pockets insided body
    labels = measure.label(binary_image, background = 0)
    l_max = largest_label_volume(labels, bg = 0)
    if l_max is not None: # There are air pockets
        binary_image[labels != l_max] = 0

    return binary_image
```

Well, the problem is that unsharp masking is really really old technique, analog version dating back to the 1930s. So even in the 1970s, there are articles about the application but not on the original method.
Where you actually can find reference: image processing books/textbooks. 

Most of the time it is just in the middle of the book, not even in a dedicated chapter, so you could cite
one or two pages from the book. On google books, you can actually look into the book, so
here are some links:

On Page 274, section 5.10.2:
https://books.google.com/books?id=JeDGn6Wmf1kC&pg=PA274&dq=image+processing+unsharp+masking&hl=en&sa=X&redir_esc=y#v=onepage&q&f=false

Example citation could be: Jayaraman, S., Esakkirajan, S., & Veerakima, T. (2009). Digital image processing, ed iii., page 274

Similarly, on page 174-175, see equation 6.38.
 https://books.google.com/books?id=YpzWCwAAQBAJ&dq=Gonzalez%2C+R.C.%2C+Woods%2C+R.E.%3A+Digital+Image+Processing%2C&q=unsharp#v=snippet&q=unsharp&f=false

Another nice here, see page 104, section 5.7:
https://books.google.com/books?id=9irSCgAAQBAJ&pg=PA104&dq=unsharp+masking+intitle:image+intitle:processing&hl=en&sa=X&redir_esc=y#v=onepage&q=unsharp%20masking%20intitle%3Aimage%20intitle%3Aprocessing&f=false

Thanks. I corrected the `np.unique` issue. 

`min_diff` can be actually smaller than the floating point precision (`np.array([1e-25]) - np.array([1e-26]) < np.finfo(a.dtype).eps`): if there are very small values `min_diff` can thus take values for which `f(x) + min_diff != f(x)` does not hold for all values `f(x)` in the image. 

So, I need to lower bound the algorithm's resolution to the worst precision I might find for the image values. And this is for floating point scikit images `np.spacing(1.0)` which is the same as `np.finfo(a.dtype).eps`. I could also use `np.spacing(img.max())` which would then allow me to apply the algorithm on images with values larger than 1.0. 

After that, I need to identify all pixels for which `R_f(f+h)-f == h` (for minima detection, R_f(g): reconstruction of g over f) or equivalently `R_f(f+h)-f >= h` (as by construction `R_f(f+h)-f` is upper bounded by `h`). I relax this condition to `R_f(f+h)-f >= h-eps`. I could also set this to `R_f(f+h)-f >= h-min_diff/2`.

I added some debug prints to `setup.py`, and, well, `sys.path` looks like this:
```
/home/mg/src/zopefoundation/zope.configuration/src
/home/mg/src/zopefoundation/zope.configuration
/home/mg/src/zopefoundation/zope.configuration/.tox/py36/lib/python36.zip
/home/mg/src/zopefoundation/zope.configuration/.tox/py36/lib/python3.6
/home/mg/src/zopefoundation/zope.configuration/.tox/py36/lib/python3.6/lib-dynload
/home/mg/opt/python36/lib/python3.6
/home/mg/src/zopefoundation/zope.configuration/.tox/py36/lib/python3.6/site-packages
```
and if you do `import zope` and look at `zope.__path__`, you see
```
['/home/mg/src/zopefoundation/zope.configuration/src/zope']
```

The source tree has a [`src/zope/__init__.py`](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.configuration/blob/master/src/zope/__init__.py) with the setuptools namespace package magic.

`/home/mg/src/zopefoundation/zope.configuration/.tox/py36/lib/python3.6/site-packages/zope` does not have a `__init__.py` because pip doesn't create one for namespace packages on Python 3.sufficientlyhighnumber because it's not needed after [PEP-420](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0420/) was implemented in Python 3.3.

This, of course, doesn't explain why `tox -e py3.4` doesn't fail.  On 3.4, `import zope` produces a `zope.__path__` that contains both locations:
```
['/home/mg/src/zopefoundation/zope.configuration/src/zope', '/home/mg/src/zopefoundation/zope.configuration/.tox/py34/lib/python3.4/site-packages/zope']
```

`run()` is only called one time, to run all the classes that have been added in one big batch. After that the TestRunner life cycle is completed.

The final log path calculation is done there because timestamp is part of log path, and there might be an arbitrarily large amount of time between constructing the test runner, adding classes, and finally calling run.

Each call to add_test_class has a different config, so it would become ambiguous which one's config would be used for logging. Rather than pick something arbitrary like the first test class's config is used for logging, I've decided to move those pieces to the constructor so there is no ambiguity.

A few things:
1) Please let's use the tag "vis-data-server" in our paths. The word "autovis" is a descriptor for only one small part of the visualization server. Our task is not to serve autovis; it's to serve vis-data-server. Every class in there (BarCharts, BoxPlots, LoessRegressions, etc.) needs to look at the aggregated CSV file produced by datatable.
2) @st-pasha , datatable needs to produce aggregated files on demand. It can't possibly produce all single column or pairwise column aggregations a priori, for obvious reasons you stated. Instead, datatable needs to produce them when vis-data-server requests a particular aggregation. If you want to precalculate some of these, the problem gets much more complex because only vis-data-server knows which ones will be needed. For Version 1, I'd suggest we stick with aggregation on demand. At the moment, DatatableDataSource points to a datatable NFF file and an aggregation is requested by a list of column names in that file over which the aggregation is to be performed. Getting that list to datatable itself may require some sort of serialization of that list of strings or use of Python. Not my area of expertise.
3) @arnocandel , thanks for your observations on the H2O aggregator. You're correct regarding the superior performance of the H2O aggregator on large files because of the use of MapReduce. When I spoke of multiple passes, that was only in reference to the option of specifying in advance the size of the aggregated file. We no longer need to do this. It would be nice if we could use a distributed algorithm inside datatable the way you did for H2O and, especially, to leverage GPUs. For a baseline, though, I'd recommend starting with the simple single-threaded algorithm I gave @oleksiyskononenko and then going to distributed for V2. That's only a recommendation, however.
3) I strongly believe that we should produce CSV files for the aggregated exemplars and corresponding aggregated members list. I've already checked in a super-fast importer for these files (which should have fewer than 500 rows). Following this architecture will give us more portability for the R and Python interfaces. 
4) The aggregated exemplar file looks like this:
Separator: comma (,)
fields: every field is a String
embedded quotes in a field are represented by "" (double quotes)
If a field has no commas or embedded quotes, it need not be surrounded by quotes
Header records:
record1: Column names (separated by commas)
record2: Column types (Strings): "integer", "real", "string", "date" (can add more types later)
record3 and subsequent records: data values represented as Strings
Missing value for a data field: two adjacent commas, whether String or numeric field
Do not use NULL, null, NaN, or other schemes for representing missing values. 
    I could parse them (plus a bunch of other symbols like "*", "?", "." used by stat packages, but that 
    would slow things down.
The vis-data-server CSV parser will pad short data rows (rows with fewer fields than record1) with missing values and truncate long rows.
Numeric data may be formatted as fixed decimal or scientific notation.
Dates are formatted as MM/DD/YYYY. The vis-data-server will parse them into a Java Calendar/Date and output results according to locale.
5) The aggregated members file looks like this:
Comma separator
for each row in the exemplar file, a list of long integers
Rows in the exemplar file are one-to-one with rows in the members file.
The members file has rows of different lengths (1 or more elements). No member row can be empty (by definition of the aggregation algorithm).

Sorry, I was making a more general statement. “core” as in “don't design for it”. You need to focus on a set of basic, solid APIs first, even if they end up a bit cumbersome as long as all intents are clear. Then, if you add sugar on them and decide to package them as standard stuff, you might very well expose them in `openfisca_core`. But the software will be much more reliable and evolutive if the abstractions are built in layers.
If you present here a choice between options and present the “how it could be after yet another evolution step”, you're taking the risk that people vote on the very last line because it is sexy, while the previous lines combination is suboptimal.

The default UDP packet size is usually 64KB but it might vary on different OS. You can consider to increase this limit.

You can also change `streaming_threshold` to a lower value so that some data is streamed out earlier:
`xray_recorder.configure(streaming_threshold=10)`

See https://github.com/aws/aws-xray-sdk-python/blob/master/aws_xray_sdk/core/recorder.py#L118. The default value is 30 which means a segment will stream out its children subsegments when the total children count reaches 30. You can change to 10 or even lower to fit your use case.

Try calling the Enthought's version of IPython not the system IPython.  `which ipython` will provide the full path to the binary.  It should be something like ~/Enthought/Canopy_32bit/User/bin/ipython, if it is not, you will want to adjust your PATH variable so that the Enthought IPython is picked up before the system Python.

The longer explanation is:

You have two Python installs on your machine:
* The system Python, likely in /usr/bin/python or /usr/local/bin/python
* The Enthough Canopy Python, I'm guessing in ~/Enthought/Canopy_32bit/User/bin/python

From the two paths in look like Py-ART got installed for both Python installs (the system Python and Enthought Python).  The system and Enthough Python interpreters use different representations for for unicode which are incompatible, extensions compiled against one version will not work with the other.  This leads to issues when the two interpreters and libraries are mixed.

Thanks for your contribution! A few things to mention:

*   We don't currently include Ubuntu as a package config option because there are setting changes specific to Ubuntu images. In particular, we will need to adjust [instance_defaults.cfg](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/compute-image-packages/blob/master/package/instance_configs.cfg) to disable parts of instance setup that should not execute. *   The new guest does replace the old packages, but gce-startup-scripts is the Ubuntu specific package name (the package is google-startup-scripts on other Linux distros).
