*   The (non-Ubuntu) packages are hosted and available in Google's cloud package repository. Packaging instructions can be found in the [package distribution](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/compute-image-packages#package-distribution) section of our README.

*   We've changed our contribution process where all pull requests should be made against the development branch. The change is mentioned in the [contributing](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/compute-image-packages#contributing) section of our README.

A high-level overview for how to get some basic ruby functionality set up is that you need to:

* Create a new type of `Target` that includes enough information to specify what you want to load into the Ruby REPL
* Create a new type of `Task` that extracts all that information from your new `Target` type and runs the REPL
* Register that `Task` with the `repl` goal

Then if you put your new `Target` in a `BUILD` file and do something like:

```bash
$ ./pants repl path/to/your:target
```

... then `pants` will:

* Check all tasks registered with the `repl` goal (including the one you just wrote)
* Provide each registered `Task` with the target and let them do whatever they want with it.  All other `Task`s will ignore your target, but your `Task` will use it to run the REPL

I'm leaving out a lot of details, but hopefully studying the other plugins will help flesh out the remaining details.

Could you post your training command and the log here? Are you using a special kind of data with very different source/target lengths? The line in your post above seems fine in that it says that your maximum observed source length or input_length is 23 tokens and we allow output lengths for
```
output_length = int(np.ceil(factor * input_length))
```
where `factor` is 
```
max_mean + (max_std * num_stds)
```
`max_mean` is the mean target/source length ratio observed in the training data ('max' because we find the maximum value if multiple models are loaded for ensembling), and `max_std=2` by default.
Disregarding the `ceil()`, your mean length ratio + 2 std (factor) should be around
```
49/23 = 2.13
```
which says that your target side data is on average twice as long as your input.

@damirda When a base backup is taken, the WAL files generated during the time when the backup process is started and when it ends are also needed to restore from a base backup. The range of the WAL files corresponding to each base backup can be found using the `--detail` option in the `backup-list` operation.

#### From the readme(https://github.com/wal-e/wal-e#backup-list):
##### wal_segment_backup_start
The wal segment number.  It is a 24-character hexadecimal number. This information identifies the timeline and relative ordering of various backups.

##### wal_segment_backup_stop
The last WAL segment file required to bring this backup into a consistent state, and thus available for hot-standby.


So, if you have all the WAL segments between this range and the base backup then it will be consistent.

> There are over thirty different suffixes classified in these two general groups of suffixes. The stemming algorithm covers only these suffixes that are appended to noun stems, since finding noun stems is essential for our information retrieval purposes.
[...]
> As mentioned before, Turkish stemmer described in this study stems only noun and nominal verb suffixes. The stemmer can be enhanced to stem all kinds of verb suffixes. In Turkish, there are over fifty suffixes that can be affixed to verbs [2]. The morphological structure of verb suffixes is more complicated than noun suffixes. Hey @hackabletype,

Thanks for filing this. Seems to me like there are two separate processes that are failing in this case: the Recipient tagging and the Box Group Type tagging. I'll address them both below.

----

In terms of the Recipient problem, right now the parser doesn't perform well with initialized names. Take a look at this simplified example:

```python
In [1]: usaddress.tag('T S ELLIOT 123 MAIN ST CANTON OH 44730-9521')
Out[1]:
(OrderedDict([('AddressNumber', 'T'),
              ('StreetNamePreDirectional', 'S'),
              ('StreetName', 'ELLIOT 123 MAIN'),
              ('StreetNamePostType', 'ST'),
              ('PlaceName', 'CANTON'),
              ('StateName', 'OH'),
              ('ZipCode', '44730-9521')]),
 'Street Address')
```

Not great. But we can reformat the initials to get the parser to catch them:

```python
# Squash the initials into one connected group
In [2]: usaddress.tag('TS ELLIOT 123 MAIN ST CANTON OH 44730-9521')

Out[2]:
(OrderedDict([('Recipient', 'TS ELLIOT'),
              ('AddressNumber', '123'),
              ('StreetName', 'MAIN'),
              ('StreetNamePostType', 'ST'),
              ('PlaceName', 'CANTON'),
              ('StateName', 'OH'),
              ('ZipCode', '44730-9521')]),
 'Street Address')

# Add dots between the initials
In [3]: usaddress.tag('T.S. ELLIOT 123 MAIN ST CANTON OH 44730-9521')

Out[3]:
(OrderedDict([('Recipient', 'T.S. ELLIOT'),
              ('AddressNumber', '123'),
              ('StreetName', 'MAIN'),
              ('StreetNamePostType', 'ST'),
              ('PlaceName', 'CANTON'),
              ('StateName', 'OH'),
              ('ZipCode', '44730-9521')]),
 'Street Address')
```

Much better!

Problem is, the recipient name issue is also messing with the PO Box parsing. According to the URISA standard, RR boxes should be categorized as a `USPSBoxGroup`. If we remove the recipient name altogether, we get the right behavior:

```python
In [4]: usaddress.tag('RR 3 BOX 9 CANTON OH 44730-9521')

Out[4]:
(OrderedDict([('USPSBoxGroupType', 'RR'),
              ('USPSBoxGroupID', '3'),
              ('USPSBoxType', 'BOX'),
              ('USPSBoxID', '9'),
              ('PlaceName', 'CANTON'),
              ('StateName', 'OH'),
              ('ZipCode', '44730-9521')]),
 'PO Box')
```

But even with a perfectly-formatted, comma-separated recipient group, this parsing fails:

```python
In [5]: usaddress.tag('THOMAS ELLIOT RR 3 BOX 9 CANTON OH 44730-9521')

RepeatedLabelError:

UNCERTAIN LABEL:  USPSBoxGroupType
```

My guess in this case is that you're at the intersection of three uncommon address features: 1) an initialized recipient name, 2) a PO Box that includes a USPS Box Group, and 3) a recipient/box group/PO Box combination. My bet is that our training data doesn't include a whole lot of examples like this one.

Our next round of training data includes a bunch more PO Box examples, so hopefully we'll start getting better behavior around that error soon. I'll update this issue once we push that up. It also might be worth training on more initialized names, too. @hackabletype, if you run into similar errors like this in the wild, be sure to let us know!

An alternate approach would be to perform TLS termination using a reverse proxy that has certificate management automation in place. https://caddyserver.com/ could be a good example of that.

The API does not have a hook to run the `match` filtering because that is only valid on the CLI.  But depending on what your goal is, there are a few ways to get around this.

1) The Firewall's `commit()` does return a job ID that you can capture, and thus do followup queries on.  Specifically:

```
job_id = fw.commit()
time.sleep(120)
cmd = 'show jobs id "{0}"'.format(job_id)
print fw.op(cmd, xml=True)
```
 -or-

2) The function `commit()` does have an optional param you can pass in, `sync=True`, that will block until the commit is finished.  The sleep I see in your code snippet above seems to imply that you are looking for this behavior.  When specifying `sync=True`, a dict is returned that gives a bit more detailed information as to the job's results.  So you could replace everything after your first print with this:

```
print fw.commit(sync=True)
```

[C:\...\]sas.exe /regserver

Hi @hermanocabral,

We don't support something like operator customization per field right now.

You could, if that was the only boolean field you wanted to filter on for this ModelAdmin, subclass the [`AdvancedFilterQueryForm` form][1] and specify your own label:

```python
class CustomLabelAdvancedFilterQueryForm(AdvancedFilterQueryForm):
    """ Build the query from field, operator and value, customizing the boolean values """
    OPERATORS = AdvancedFilterQueryForm.OPERATORS + (
        ("istrue", _("Is Male")),
        ("isfalse", _("Is Female")),
    )
```

Replacing the above form means you'd also have to construct your own formset, and use it in `AdvancedFilterForm. initialize_form `

Though to be clear, I would probably make a field such as gender selection, an int/char field with 2 choices, which would be properly rendered by our form.

Hi,

I think the answer is going to depend upon what kind of endpoints you're working with. You can query for shared endpoints that you have access to by using the `endpoint_search()` function and passing in the search filter `shared-with-me`.

That will only return "shared" endpoints though. Non-shared or "host" endpoints are different in that in order to access them you have to provide a set of credentials at the time of access. That means that the Globus Transfer API (that's the API you're talking to with the `endpoint_search()` function) isn't able to tell you which of those types of endpoints you might be able to authenticate with.

Does that make sense?

Still, the "local" temporary table (prefixed with a single `#` is supposed to be visible throughout the duration of the connection:

> Local temporary tables have a single number sign (#) as the first character of their names; they are visible only to the current connection for the user, and they are deleted when the user disconnects from the instance of SQL Server.
Source: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/relational-databases/tables/tables

- Turning off autocommit
- Explicitly committing the temp table creation with `SQLEndTran`
- Freeing the statement handle and creating a new one (this works with direct execution but not with prepare/execute)

An experimental flag `featureless_minimization_disabled_feature_flag` has been added with which the `features` of packages can be completely ignored. This helps with issues like this one that happen on Windows due to packages from `conda-forge` that still have `features: vc`.

I think there's a reasonable overhead in specifying a new yaml and building a new package for each set of optional dependencies - yes it can work but I think it would be much easier to specify in the same yaml as the required dependencies IMHO.

My suggestion would be to allow optional dependencies to simply be labelled sections under the build or run requirements. The labelled sections then specify the dependencies for that label - e.g. for the hypothetical meta.yaml below:
```yaml
package:
  name: mypackage
  version: 1.0.0

  requirements:
    build:
      - python

    run:
      - python
      - numpy

      pyqt:
        - pyqt

      docs:
        - sphinx
        - numpydoc >= 0.5.0

      test:
        - nose
        - mock
```

Only python and numpy would get installed with:
```
conda install mypackage
```
to install all the optional deps you would use
```
conda install mypackage[all]
```
...which would be equivalent to
```
conda install mypackage[pyqt, docs, test]
```
To install mypackage with just the pyqt dependency would be
```
conda install mypackage[pyqt]
```

Conveniently this would also solve my own issue (https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1665) where I want to be able to independently install the test deps.

@msarahan thank you for summarizing my thoughts. We are definitely looking at ways to address the undesirable feature-specific behavior. This PR represents great work @mbargull, and I'm grateful for it.

I am not convinced yet that we can take the risk of modifying the current feature behavior. A lot of work, including many user-facing failures, sadly, went into its development. Its current position in the optimization food chain was an explicit output of that iteration. That is not to say that I cannot be convinced otherwise. For one thing, I'm not a BDFL here, by any means, so I can be overruled. And for another, if we can do enough verification that a feature change will not introduce regressions in other areas, then I could be swayed.

But one thing I want to be very clear about is if `conda` is producing a _broken_ environment---that is, an environment contains packages that are not compatible with each other---then features are _by definition_ not to blame, and focusing on the feature behavior is at least somewhat of a red herring.

We talk a lot about the multiple optimization passes that conda does to select the best set of packages to install. There has been plenty of debate, adjustment, and tweaking of what order those optimization passes go in, and we can do more! But the optimization passes come into play _only_ after the family of _compatible_ packages has been identified. In other words, the optimization passes are designed to select the best combination from all _acceptable_ combinations. 

So while there may indeed be some disagreement about whether recipe A or recipe B is best, the optimization passes are _assuming_ that both A and B are at least _functional_. If this assumption is not valid, then there is something broken in the dependency metadata that we need to fix. We should _not_ be using the optimization passes to enforce correctness.

That said, features do occupy a strange middle ground. They are intended to help us select between different installation alternatives, and they have a flavor of both "correctness" and "optimization" to them. Features are very hard to reason about and that's why we are actually trying to _move away_ from them when we can, in favor of things like [variants](https://gist.github.com/mcg1969/20667002c79f702e3c7003c81b5f24c8). So I don't want to claim that features have _no_ blame in the undesirable behavior we are seeing. 

However, I think we would benefit by making sure we truly understand all of the issues here.  But at least one bug report seems to claim that features are causing a broken environment to be created. That is definitely _not_ correct. That needs to be identified and solved as a separate issue.

I'm sorry that this is a lot of words and no action! But I did at least want to share my thinking, if nothing else as evidence that we're banging our heads against the proverbial wall on this one trying to figure out the right course forward.

> Things like Pyodide look really attractive for solving these kinds of problems.

Pyodide and Iodide are explicitly targetting the use cases you describe.

> what is a compiler for WebAssembly. 

I think perhaps you are confusing a compiler with a toolchain.  For supporting legacy C code written for POSIX operating systems, a full toolchain like `emsdk` is the best option.  The other options only make sense for code written explicitly for WebAssembly or that makes no cstdlib or system calls.

> what an activated compiler looks like (e.g. what environment variables do we set, how do we configure it, etc.). We can also support multiple options this way if needed.

I think `emsdk` ticks all the boxes and I don't see a need to reinvent that wheel.  Overtime, `emsdk` will move to the more modern compilation technologies you mention under the hood.

> >   There are some problems to solve on Chrome given its requirement for asynchronous compilation...

> Guess you are referring to issue ( kripken/emscripten#6633 )?

Yes.  That's only a partial solution as it requires loading all dynamic libraries you might ever want to load up front, which could become a memory usage issue (and currently is with `pandas`).

> >   Emscripten allows filesystems to be overlayed...

> Interesting. So how are these packages hosted? What does the format look like? How are versions tracked?

It doesn't really deal with hosting or version tracking.  It's just a virtual filesystem layer that makes the Wasm code think it has access to a POSIX file system.  You can load files into that virtual filesystem using any number of overlays.  Pyodide, for example, has a filesystem it loads containing only CPython, and then if you load the Numpy package, it loads an overlay containing Numpy.  The file format is made up of two files: a `.js` file that has the metadata about the files and the code to load them into emscripten's virtual filesystem, and a `.data` file which is literally all of the files concatenated together.  See [file_packager.py](https://github.com/kripken/emscripten/blob/incoming/tools/file_packager.py) for the script that generates these files.

Where does the "single package" fit in here? 

In any case, while fixing conda's version comparison code a few weeks ago, I got the strong impression that build string comparison is very much ad hoc, and one should not base significant version resolution decisions on build strings in their current form. That's one rationale behind my suggestion to add suffixes to version numbers, like `1.2.3.vc11`.

>  However, it introduces pinning issues that might be annoying.

Which pinning issues?

> Any library needs both features.

In fact, I believe that any compiled Python module that #includes numpy headers needs at least three features: the compiler version, the Python version, and the numpy version. The ABIs of these packages differ between versions, and any mismatched installation *will* crash sooner or later.

> In the current system, track-features lives in Python - nowhere else.

Since you already introduced `msvc_runtime` packages, they should track the appropriate `vc?` as well because this feature is also relevant (in fact, required) in pure C-packages like libtiff that don't depend on Python at all.

>  Python 2.7 tracks vc9

True, but we needed a Python 2.7 that tracks vc11 because vc9 is too old to compile our C++ packages, and we cannot yet switch to Python 3. Since we couldn't find recipes with that property, we created them from scratch (by now about 130, see https://github.com/ilastik/ilastik-build-conda/tree/toolset-config).

> Your jinja2 stuff would make this much nicer, but we have a lot of work to do internally to modernize recipes to use jinja2. That's probably the long-term fix.

The recipes linked above already contain a full implementation of a (preliminary) variant of the jinja2 stuff for Windows. We would like to finalize this design and extend it to the other platforms as soon as possible, but work is effectively stalled due to https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/1727, https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/662, https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/664, https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/666, https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/667. I replaced the `conda.std(out|err).raw` loggers with `conda.std(out|err)log`. It just did not make sense to have them placed under the `conda.std(out|err)` loggers in the hierarchy since they are really separate loggers which behave differently w.r.t. logging levels. That is, having different levels from the start and only one set of them being silenced in `conda.cli.main`.
IMHO they should be replaced by the `conda.std(out|err)` loggers where appropriate (=where they should always have the same level), and otherwise be renamed to better reflect their difference to the other loggers.
When being replaced by `conda.std(out|err)`, their previous behavior of not outputting a trailing newline can now be replicated with, e.g.,
```python
getLogger("conda.stdout").info("log message", extra=dict(terminator=""))
```
or
```python
stdoutlog_same_line = LoggingAdapter(getLogger("conda.stdout"), extra=dict(terminator=""))
stdoutlog_same_line.info("log message")

I was also asking about the case where 'x' is a carray. It seems that at worst, adding (or any arithmetic ops) 2 carrays, ``x + y`` means:
- decompress x, decompress y
- add
- recompress the result (using what cparams?)

Which is probably very expensive even if you're calling more costly functions like log, and decompress/recompress would easily dominate the time of addition.

And if I wanted to do ``x + y + z``, that would compound the decompress/recompress. So maybe...
- handle this case but only if compression level = 0
    - even here it's not obvious--maybe most natural to let numpy do the math but for GB of data, that's a lot of copying back & forth btwn bcolz and numpy
- move compression into a more inner loop and chunk it so that we decompress some portion of x, y, z, do the ops, and then recompress only the final result (all intermediates stay uncompressed)
    - maybe do it lazily? so ops don't evaluate right now but only when the user asks for it. So 'z = x + y' and then 'z += 2' becomes ``z = x + y + 2`` internally and only evaluated when the user requires it. Would probably want an expression tree or parser that knows how to eliminate constants, common subexpressions, etc... which is sounding big and complex.
- or is there a compression scheme that is amenable to arithmetic operations? (like somehow I can add, multiply, etc. without decompress/recompress)

A big difference between zarr and bcolz is the array versus column approach. While I understand that for specific use cases (especially in predictive modeling) you need to have an entire data array directly available, but the fact is that for most business intelligence use cases you tend to rely heavily on a columnar approach (where adding more columns to a table would not impact performance and they can be added and dropped independently).
E.g.: we construct a pandas dataframe by aggregating measures to a specific number of groupby columns in parallel over hundreds of bcolz "shards" that in total contain billions of records and then use that end result for reporting and machine learning purposes.
Going from a columnar approach back to the old array/table approach would be a major step back in performance and usability.

> I cannot think of any objective difference between your current proposal and what I implemented. It is a matter of preference if you want to group files by libraries or view the library as a property of the file.

My use case is to generate a list of files to be used in a TCL script that runs a synthesis tool. E.g.

``` tcl
for {set i 0} {$i < [llength $libs]} {incr i} {
  eval "set alib $[lindex $libs $i]"
  foreach j $alib {
    add_input_file [lindex $dirs $i]/hdl/$j.vhd -format {VHDL_2008} -work [lindex $libs $i]
  }
}
```

In order to get all the files that belong to a library, with you current proposal the name must be parsed. So, all the list/array must be processed to get/discard the files corresponding to a library. E.g. to ignore those in library 'test' or 'sim'.

A different context where grouping file/test according to the libraries is useful, is to show a tree view in a GUI. If the user wants to see all of them without hierarchy, it is easier for a tool to append fields rather than split them. However, as you say, changing how names are created might be a breaking change.

Note that these suggested changes are related to:

- Using a JSON config file and a 'generic' `run.py`. See [`config.json`](https://github.com/1138-4EB/vunit/blob/json_cfg_run/examples/vhdl/array_axis_vcs/config.json) and [`cfg_run.py`](https://github.com/1138-4EB/vunit/blob/json_cfg_run/examples/vhdl/array_axis_vcs/cfg_run.py).
- Exporting `VUnit.get_source_files`, `VUnit.get_compile_order` and/or `VUnit.get_implementation_subset` to JSON. See #352. E.g.:

``` python
files_dict = dict()
for x in vu.get_compile_order():
   try:

   except:
      files_dict[x.library.name]=[x.name]

with open('compile_files.json', 'w') as outfile:

@Anshapar How did you get your cert? The problem for me was the CLI uses Python. That environment variable tells Python where to look for it's trusted certs. Try using chrome and following these steps to save your root cert.

- Open chrome dev tools

- Click Security tab

- Click View Certificate button

- On the Certification Path tab click the highest node in the tree

- Click View Certificate

- On the Details tab click the Copy to File button

- Make sure to select Base-64 encoded X.509 (.CER)

- Save the file somewhere on your drive (ex. C:\certs\my_root.cer)

- Run the command 'set REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE=C:\certs\my_root.cer' from a command prompt

- Run the command 'echo %REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE%' to verify it set correctly

Thanks for reporting this @tjprescott and @anzaman!

Firstly, I've verified that it works on Linux (verified on Ubuntu) and OS X.

After investigation, it looks like you've run into the Maximum Path Length Limitation on Windows. It just happens that the file path of `express_route_circuit_creation_client_enums.cpython-35.pyc` exceeds the 260 character limit.

Looking at the filepath of the file not found, `C:\Users\alzam\AppData\Local\Temp\pip-3bug3zw9-uninstall\users\alzam\appdata\local\programs\python\python35\lib\site-packages\azure\cli\command_modules\network\mgmt_express_route_circuit\lib\models\__pycache__\express_route_circuit_creation_client_enums.cpython-35.pyc` is 268 characters long.

> In the Windows API (with some exceptions discussed in the following paragraphs), the maximum length for a path is MAX_PATH, which is defined as 260 characters. 
https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/aa365247(VS.85).aspx#maxpath

- Related to [Upgrading pip fails on Windows when install path is too long #3055](https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/3055)
- Related to [pip install fails to remove its temporary directory on Windows when it contains a deep folder hierarchy. #2892](https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/2892)
- Python bug tracking this - [Windows: support path longer than 260 bytes using "\\?\" prefix](https://bugs.python.org/issue18199)

---

Another repro that excludes `az component` from this issue is:
```
$ pip install azure-cli
$ pip install --upgrade azure-cli --extra-index-url https://azureclinightly.blob.core.windows.net/packages
```

On Windows, if the filepath happens to be too long, you'll see a `FileNotFoundError` error from pip.

Hi @lucaluca, sorry to hear that you had to deal with this problem on Azure Web Apps. 😢 

According to what I understood from reading that issue, this problem is really caused by the older version of pip which is pre-installed on Azure Web Apps and cannot install Cryptography properly. However, there is this [workaround](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/issues/219#issuecomment-270867174) to upgrade the pip. As far as I know this is not a problem present on other Windows platforms that have newer versions of pip. The Cryptography wheel package on Windows is a statically linked build, so newer pips should install it just fine, and users do not need to build anything natively.

Please let me know whether I understood this correctly. If yes, could you please modify the documentation to point out that:
1) there might be a problem on Azure Web Apps to install Cryptography

    processes.append(Process(target=mp_queue_feeder_function, args=(q_object,...)))
for p in processes:
    p.start()
for p in processes:
    p.join()

So it's a single instance that's used by multiple processes in Python on a single machine. Of course, this works too with multiple machines running only one process/thread feeding on a QueueService object, but we want to take advantage of multiple processes within a single VM as well.

The intended way to handle this is to specify `language_version` for each python hook in your repository as [documented here](http://pre-commit.com/#overriding-language-version)

pre-commit *defaults* to making virtualenvs in the same interpreter as it is running, but can make environments for any version of the language.

I have some pie-in-the-sky ideas to make this work better with a configuration of something like this:

```yaml
default_language_versions:
    python: python2.7
repos:
    -    repo: ...
          ...
```

```yaml
repos:
-   repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks.git
    sha: v0.9.1
    hooks:
    -   id: trailing-whitespace

@asvetlov I'm proposing that we strictly follow the RFC, and forbids the handler returns a 1xx response. If the handler returns a 1xx response, aiohttp should raise an exception and return a 500 response to the client. This prevents the indefinite block. I can work on it after we come to a conclusion.

How to add customized processing on 1xx responses - for example, allowing the server to return customized 1xx responses, and allowing the client to retrieve and process the 1xx responses - are another question. It will need some more complicated design, and I don't think it is  very useful. The client side should not be very difficult to implement, but the server side may be harder.

@SantjagoCorkez for your question. Most designs choose to use normal response code (200) with a JSON body for result. Use HEAD instead of GET is not very helpful, because the response is small enough to be included in a single packet (about 1.4KB). If you only query the server less then once per second, the extra processing will not be a bottleneck, and it is quite easy to implement in any language (even bash). And the last request will directly get the final result. If you use HEAD, you will need another GET request after the HEAD request succeeded.

If you really want a status code based solution, I would suggest 304 (Not Modified), or 412 (Precondition Failed). They are compatible to most HTTP client implementations. Or you may define your own status code like 299 or 269.

> an application-specific header is then a workaround for what should be exactly covered with 102 Processing without any headers at all

`102 Processing` is a obsoleted status code defined by RFC 2518
https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2518#page-59
10.1 102 Processing

>    The 102 (Processing) status code is an interim response used to
>    inform the client that the server has accepted the complete request,
>    but has not yet completed it.  This status code SHOULD only be sent
>    when the server has a reasonable expectation that the request will
>    take significant time to complete. As guidance, if a method is taking
>    longer than 20 seconds (a reasonable, but arbitrary value) to process
>    the server SHOULD return a 102 (Processing) response. The server MUST
>    send a final response after the request has been completed.
> 
>    Methods can potentially take a long period of time to process,
>    especially methods that support the Depth header.  In such cases the
>    client may time-out the connection while waiting for a response.  To
>    prevent this the server may return a 102 (Processing) status code to
>    indicate to the client that the server is still processing the
>    method.

RFC 2518 suggests to use it as an interim response before the final response to prevent the connection become timeout. If you are planning to use it, you will need to periodically yield a 102 response before the final 200 response, which is not supported by aiohttp now.

RFC compliance might be bigger problem than you think. If you put your server behind nginx, it might think your request is not completed, and refuse to send more requests to the connection. And other client implementations (some in other languages) cannot use your API. HTTP is a very complicated standard, and different implementations vary a lot, so usually we want to use the most common functions to avoid incompatibilities.

Thanks for reporting, this should be fixed. It turned out the solution was very simple: just to switch around the order of importing the code and calling `get_event_loop()` so it gets the right loop.

The following works, it just requires that `asyncio.set_event_loop_policy(uvloop.EventLoopPolicy())` is in module context so it's executed when the module is imported.

```python
from aiohttp import web
import asyncio
import uvloop

async def handle(request):
    return web.Response(text='testing more')

asyncio.set_event_loop_policy(uvloop.EventLoopPolicy())

app = web.Application()
app.router.add_get('/', handle)
```

The main argument now is that if CommonMark extensions do happen and we adopt them, then we would be breaking notebooks twice instead of once. To be clear, adopting markdown-it + extensions *is not* adopting CommonMark, it is adopting yet another markdown dialect defined by a single implementation. That's not to say that I'm opposed to adopting markdown-it, but doing so should mean that we are giving up on the prospect of actual CommonMark extensions in the vaguely near future. Of course, if we stick with CommonMark-derived implementations (as we probably should), the domain of those incompatibilities ought to be confined to the areas affected by extensions, and not basic things like spaces in/around headings, like we've seen in the past.

I think the fact that updates are lost is not so much of a problem; when I have long-running programs, I frequently call IPython display functions and/or generate new output anyway. That is, the lack of buffering is something that we can fairly easily handle in user code (of course, it would still be nicer if we didn't have to, but it's just not that important).

The first and foremost problem is that when connecting to the notebook server with a new browser, the new browser never gets any updates. So even if the display is refreshed and/or new output appears, it still doesn't show up.

As far as I can tell, the notebook server simply doesn't send output to all browser, but only to the one that actually originally executed the cell. Fixing that would fix most of the problems for long-running notebooks.

> The problem with that approach, is that you actually need to read the hash which can be extremely huge, which can be a pain, especially on samba.

Not sure how it can be "really huge", a notebook json file that is semi-large will break the notebook anyways.

I don't think it's so bad for newcomers - the default experience is with it opening a browser directly on launch, which works (as far as we know). The problems are mainly for existing users who already have a different way of doing things which doesn't work with this. Like yourself and the hidden no-browser setting.

The login page (which you see if you miss the token) will point users to `jupyter notebook list` as of 4.3.1.

The only other thing I can think of to do is to special case connections which say they're coming from `localhost` or `127.0.0.1` (and the correct port). I.e. check the origin against what the server expects it to be, rather than comparing two values sent by the browser. But I'm not wild on this, because:

- Adding an exception to any security mechanism is a risk, because someone might find a way to exploit that exception. The flaw that prompted adding this mechanism highlighted just how complex browsers and web security are. More special cases makes it harder to be sure we're doing things right.
- Special casing specific origins means more surprising behaviour when you're legitimately accessing it at a different domain.

> the cookie is not set on both localhost and 127.0.0.1 which is confising.

I understand that it's confusing, but from the browser's point of view, those are different domains. We [can't set a cookie for another domain](http://stackoverflow.com/a/6761443/434217).

> I can see a problem with identical-path in many kernels.

I don't expect this to be a problem. Any resource fetched from a kernel should necessarily be served from a kernel-specific path. So when kernel K is asked for resource R, the server maps it to `/K/R`, not `/R`, so kernels are not capable of collision with each other.

I do think if we are going as far as making the Kernels be responsible for static resources via messages, the most logical way to do that is to proxy requests to the Kernels themselves, and expect Kernels to run an HTTP server to just serve the files. HTTP already has all the features we are describing here, I think.

Sure, so I'm using `pytest` which is triggered via `python setup.py test`. This looks at `setup.py` to determine which packages are required for testing, and then installs them into an adjacent `.eggs` folder, or into the project root itself (depending on whether you're using py2/py3). This allows you to run a project test suite without having to install its dependancies globally, and is considered standard practice afaik. 

Are you aware of any better approaches? I've double checked the docs for pytest and setuptools, but found nothing to suggest otherwise.

Overall, I don't think that it's optimal situation that we have (at least) 3 competing validation/serialization frameworks out there: `schematics`, `marshmallow` and `voluptuous`. Yeah, each of them has a slightly different focus, but as a silly customer I would love to see the power of all in just one package.

At the moment I'm using marshmallow for parameter validation (for it has built-in integration via `webargs`), voluptuous in tests (doing some asserts with it because it has the least-boilerplate syntax) and schematics for remaining code (compared to marshmallow, I access the data via `Model` instance, no need for custom construction). I think first schematics, then discovered voluptuous and then marhsmallow/webargs, which left me using different libraries for different tasks... yeah, one could say that's the way to go ("use the right tool for the task") but I don't quite agree.

```python
In [1]: a = b = np.arange(3)

In [2]: np.add.outer(a, b)
Out[2]:
array([[0, 1, 2],
       [1, 2, 3],
       [2, 3, 4]])

In [3]: a[:,None] + b
Out[3]:
array([[0, 1, 2],
       [1, 2, 3],
       [2, 3, 4]])
```

> Is it possible to just maintain recent "unstable state" instead of a full unstable indexer and then throw away / update that unstable state as blocks come in?

The logic required to accept unstable but valid state is basically the same as the validation logic required to run an indexer.  I was thinking we'd just do something like this:

```
index_block_heights = [...]
blockstack_dir = ...
stable_height = bitcoin.getblockcount() - MIN_CONFS_REQUIRED

# index most-stable to least-stable
index_block_heights.sort()

# build up indexes at each index height
for block_height in index_block_heights:
   unstable_state_dir = os.path.join(blockstack_dir, "unstable-{}".format(block_height)
   sync_virtualchain(block_height, working_dir=unstable_state_dir)

# discard less-stable indexes that have diverged from more-stable indexes
all_index_heights = [stable_height] + index_block_heights
all_index_heights.reverse()

for i in xrange(0, len(all_index_heights)-1):
   more_stable_state_dir = os.path.join(working_dir, "unstable-{}".format(index_block_heights[i+1]))
   less_stable_state_dir = os.path.join(working_dir, "unstable-{}".format(index_block_heights[i]))

   if is_empty_dir(more_stable_state_dir):
      continue

   if is_empty_dir(less_stable_state_dir):
      continue

   more_stable_ch = get_consensus_hash(more_stable_state_dir)
   less_stable_ch = get_consensus_hash(less_stable_state_dir)

   if more_stable_ch != less_stable_ch:
      # less-stable state was on a minority fork
      # blow it away and try again from the stable state
      shutil.rmtree(less_stable_state)
      bootstrap_from_existing_state(more_stable_state, less_stable_state)
```

Queries like `lookup` or `whois` would optionally take an index height they'd like to check, allowing the client to trade stability for faster results.

I'm really starting to think that it might be good to also implement `nailgun.config.ServerConfig.__repr__`. It'll mean a bit more work, but the tests should pass without further changes of any sort, and really - server configuration data is an integral part of an entity object. Not including that information in an entity's `__repr__` is disingenuous.

Unfortunately, the server is very inconsistent about whether it returns a hash of information about a referenced entity or just an ID or list of IDs. This sometimes leads to some pretty epic hacking around. Check out `HostGroup.read`:

```
        Normalize the data received from the server.

        The server returns data in this format::

            {
                'id': 15,
                'name': 'name',
                'title': 'title',
                'subnet_id': null,
                'subnet_name': null,
                'operatingsystem_id': null,
                'operatingsystem_name': null,
                …
            }

        Normalize data to this format::

            {
                'id': 15,
                'name': 'name',
                'title': 'title',
                'subnet': {'id': …},
                'operatingsystem': {'id': …},
                …
            }
```

The current solution is to deal with these inconsistencies on a case-by-case basis. Here's a pretty typical example of how to do that:

```python
    def read(self, entity=None, attrs=None, ignore=()):
        """Compensate for the weird structure of returned data."""
        if attrs is None:
            attrs = self.read_json()
        # The server returns an ID for this attr instead of a dict of data.
        gpg_key_id = attrs.pop('gpg_key_id')
        if gpg_key_id is None:
            attrs['gpg_key'] = None
        else:
            attrs['gpg_key'] = {'id': gpg_key_id}
        return super(Repository, self).read(entity, attrs, ignore)
```

Writing boilerplate code like this all over the place sucks, of course. A nice enhancement would be to add logic in to the `EntityReadMixin.read` method that would deal with cases like this.

Anyway, what should be done? Declare a `OneToOneField`:

```python
'dns_id': entity_fields.OneToOneField(SmartProxy),
```

And then add in whatever logic is necessary to make `create()` and `read()` both work correctly. You can test the `read()` method by adding several lines to `ReadTestCase.test_attrs_arg_v1` and `ReadTestCase.test_entity_ids`. There are currently no unit tests for the `create()` methods.

I was testing using v12.1.    I can confirm with native REST(requests) that using PATCH only updates the parameters in the request.  

If I am reading the update code properly, it looks like it adds the entire object dictionary to the request(from the load method, minus collections and a few other things it removes prior to the request)   If you do this, it will still set the 'custom' flag and break inheritance.   For objects with inheritance like ssl profiles, you need to make sure only the options that have changed are part of the request, otherwise you will break the inheritance. 

Example:

Start with a ClientSSL Profile with Secure Renegotiation set to require, inherited from the parent clientssl.

```
s = requests.session()
r = s.get(baseurl + '/tm/ltm/profile/client-ssl/~Common~test')
```

r.json()['secureRenegotiation'] has a value of u'require'

Now PATCH the same value in.

```
data = {'secureRenegotiation' : 'require' }
s.patch(baseurl + '/tm/ltm/profile/client-ssl/~Common~test', data = json.dumps(data))
r = s.get(baseurl + '/tm/ltm/profile/client-ssl/~Common~test')
```

The value of r.json()['secureRenegotiation'] is still require, however it is no longer inherited, the check box is checked in the WebUI, and the option is explicitly set in the bigip.conf

We are going to need update to be a little smarter, or perhaps have another way to modify profile objects types that have inheritance.  Either that or I am wrong about how update works, in which case please correct me.

In regards to the size, in Node we can have some scripts that are a few KB to the do the same job in .Net is 10-20mb because of the tree of dependencies that are pulled in Microsoft .Net Core framework and externally. For example, we may only use 1 tiny enum from a dependency dll and it bings in the whole 5mb dll instead of only the code for the 1 enum that is < 1KB. Our function sizes in .Net Core are on average 100's of times the size of the equivalent in Node because of the 'publish' command bringing in whole dlls, and things we don't need or will ever user - and the code never references. Traditional systems where space was not a concern this is fine, however for serverless where cold-start is most important and every large chunk of unrequired code slows down the loading of the function, it would be good to get dll's cut-down and optimized to be smaller and faster, or removed all together if the code never uses classes/methods/properties from that dll (I am particularly talking about dependencies of dependencies).

OK, here are a few approaches that I think make sense:

* Keep "existing directives take precedence" as the default behaviour, but add a comment like `# Set by LetsEncryptPythonClient" to each directive that we add, so they can be updated later.
* Switch to "new directives take precedence", and then add a UI option (we can do that in another PR) to disable that if the user wants to.  Directive comments are still nice in this case, but less necessary.
* Continue putting all of our security directives in our own separate file, and deal with the issue by commenting out any directives that conflict with ours.  If we want to support high-security and high-compatibility ciphersuite options, we can make two different config files, and include the one that the user requested.

I think any of these paths are workable, and I'm willing to merge whichever one the nginx team thinks is most elegant.

You are right that the default auto commit kicks in before passing the message to the application rather than when the application is done processing the message.
But there is a way around that without having to do manual commits.

There are actually two layers of offset commit, first there is the store_offsets() that stores (in client memory) the offset to be committed, then there's the actual commit which commits the stored offset. store_offsets() is run automatically for each message just prior to passing the message to the application, which breaks your at-least-once guarantee.

So, what you do is disable the automatic offset store by setting `enable.auto.offset.store=false` and then manually call `store_offsets()` after full processing and delivery of a message has succeeded.
Since the producer may deliver messages out of order (when using multiple partitions) you might want to make sure you don't store an older offset than what you've already stored, to avoid commits going backwards (you need to keep track of last stored offset per partition yourself).

OK, I've thought this over and I'm thinking that we'll start by adding an `order_strict` flag, defaulting to `False` in the `parserinfo` object. If `yearfirst` or `dayfirst` is specified (i.e. not `None`), the flag will have no effect, otherwise, it will throw a `ValueError` if the `order_strict` flag is set to `False`.

I also think that the more sensible default is that `order_strict` be set to `True`, since if you're actually going through the trouble of specifying whether year or day comes first, then you probably don't want it to silently ignore that if the number it gets is wrong - please let me know if you disagree about this, because I don't actually use the parameter myself. If there's agreement that `True` is the sensible default, I'll add in a `FutureWarning` that the default will change, and the default will change in a future version (we already have a few `DeprecationWarning`s floating around, so I figure we'll accumulate them over the next few feature releases, then do a major compatibility-breaking 3.0 release in a year or so that actually acts on the threats).

> I haven't looked at the leapday logic closely, and would want to be extra careful around DST transitions.

The DST transition stuff is more or less irrelevant here because the semantics of any sort of addition in non-monotonic zones is not well-defined. Doing the same thing `timedelta` does will be sufficient.

> What do you mean by "calendar" delta? Is it distinct from a timedelta?

The same thing that happens in `relativedelta` now if you specify no "absolute" components. It's an offset in civil time, not a fixed duration. In some ways `timedelta` is a sort of hybrid animal because of its arithmetic semantics, but one of the critical differences is that any `timedelta` can be represented as an offset in seconds. `calendardelta` can have `months` and `years` (neither of which is a fixed period of time), and even such things like `weekday=MO(+2)` (e.g. "2 mondays away").

I have made the documentation including more details. However, I am not sure using which strategy to handle the case. There are two selectable choices:
1. make "nInputDim" a mandatory parameter for these modules
    Drawback: the user have to calculate matrix dimensions and understand `nInputDim`
2. add 'val batch: Boolean = false' to the AbstractModule. If the user want to use batch, they can set the batch mode to true. In this case `nInputDim` may be need to be removed, because the modules decides whether to do batch operations by the `batch` parameters. If `batch` and `nInputDim` exists simultaneously, the module may not know decide by which signal. In this case, we can set `batch` to true in the Optimizer class.
   DrawBack: the user have to set batch mode to true, if they want to use batch. they may not know or forget to do these.

There is no high-level literature, but the source code is not particularly hard to read. See [th_rewriter.cpp](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/ast/rewriter/th_rewriter.cpp), which applies some theory-independent simplification rules and then calls out to the various theory-rewriters, for instance `m_b_rw`, the [bool_rewriter](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/ast/rewriter/bool_rewriter.cpp).

Got it. It's an off-by-one error in lines [80](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/util/small_object_allocator.cpp#L80) and [99](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/util/small_object_allocator.cpp#L99): If `size` is exactly `SMALL_OBJ_SIZE - (1 << PTR_ALIGNMENT)` then the increment on `slot_id` will result in an out-of-bounds access in `m_free_list`. I'd suggest using `>=` instead of `>`.

There is no dedicated tactic for QF_FPABV yet because that combination hasn't appeared in benchmarks yet. This means that Z3 will fall back to the most general solver, which supports everything (the 'smt' tactic). When you enable verbose output, e.g., -v:10, you'll see that it says 

    (simplifier ... )
    (smt ...)

While for the first query it says

    ...
    (fpa2bv ...
    ...
    (ackermannize ...
    ...
    (sat-....

If it's the floats that break things, you can rewrite them into bit-vectors up front by rolling your own custom tactic, e.g., by replacing `(check-sat)` with

    (check-sat-using (then simplify fpa2bv qfaufbv))

(where `qfaufbv` is whatever tactic you were using before the addition of floats). 

We also have recently added tactics that rewrite bit-vector arrays into bit-vector functions which then can be ackermannized, so a first draft QF_FPABV tactic for your type of problem could be as follows:

    (check-sat-using (then simplify
                     fpa2bv
                     bvarray2uf
                     ackermannize_bv
                     (using-params simplify :elim_and true)
                     bit-blast sat))

which solves the problem even faster than the original FP problem was solved.

> regarding b), I guess it's better to support it since it doesn't really add any overhead, does it? Nor does it make the code any more difficult to understand (or does it).

Not a lot. It's just that if we don't support it, we can handle the distinction between `scale` and `size` it in the constructor and set `self.size` to an integer tuple in either case. I don't think that MIT can be called "standard" in the Python Community... Standard is something that "almost" everyone uses.  A naive look at pypi shows that MIT is about 40% which is larger than the other OSI License tags but doesn't rise to the level of standard (The GNU family of licenses, for instance, is 25% of the total by the same naive metric).  (A datapoint for later: All MPL versions only total 0.5% of OSI Licenses on Pypi.)    (Google search is leading me to believe that in the OSS world at large, MIT and GNU family are neck and neck at about 32% of projects a piece).

Also note, Python programs can be distributed in non-source form.  Byte code will run fine without corresponding source, for instance.  There are various obfuscators and "freezers" as well (pex being one of the most well-known).

Third note: a few of the mentioned murkiness of the LGPL are not murky at all (for instance, copy and pasting is not murky... if the work is copyrightable, that's a license violation).  The majority of the murkiness mentioned are problems with LGPLv2 but are clarified by LGPLv3.

Those clarifications out of the way, in addition to being okay with LGPL (any version), I may be okay with the MPL but I have some reservations.  My goal is to put my work under a copyleft license so that changes related to that work are also copyleft.  I don't mind unrelated work which benefits from my projects being under non-copyleft or proprietary licenses. For that reason, I license all of my library code as LGPL rather than GPL.  MPLv2.0 and LGPLv3 appear to be the left foot and right foot treading on the line of "my-work" vs "your-work" so if most of the contributors here *prefer* the "MPLv2.0, compatible with secondary licenses" over the LGPLv3, I'd probably go along but if a significant number prefer the LGPL, I would plant myself in that camp, too. (Being clear, I mean, they would rather have this project licensed under "MPLv2.0, compatible with secondary licenses" rather than LGPL.   I want to know that "everyone wants to do this" rather than "everyone is willing to do this" [for some definition of everyone])

My reservations about the MPLv2.0 vs the LGPLv3:

* MPLv2.0 is a more complex license.
  * It attempts to pull many features of a dual licensing scenario inside of a single license.
  * It has multiple versions which are not alike in spirit or in practice.  MPLv1.x is not copyleft whereas MPLv2.0 is.  MPLv2.0 itself has two versions, one of which is GPL compatible ("compatible with secondary licenses") and one of which is not ("incompatible with secondary licenses").
  * Some terms (for instance, "secondary licenses") don't have the standard English meaning.
* MPLv2.0 is less popular than the GNU family of licenses.  License proliferation and license familiarity are real problems.
* The MPLv2.0 sells itself as being a per-file copyleft as opposed to LGPL being a per-work copyleft.  This has both pros and cons.
  * Per-file is grounded in concrete objects that makes it easier to understand what is covered versus what is not.
  * However, if you actually care about copyleft, some concept of per-work is necessary.  You can circumvent MPL's copyleft simply by placing your changes into a separate file from the MPL'd code.  The obvious way this can be used is for adding features (which is on the edge where some will think it's a good thing and others, a bad thing) but even bugfixes can be made under a different license via monkeypatching, subclassing, and otherwise overriding the base code from outside of the file.

Looking at my reservations above, I see that I share many of the same criteria for licensing as @tony, however, I do include copyleft as one of my criteria and I do place that criteria at the top of my hierarchy.

A final note... because of the necessity of getting sign off from everyone in order to relicense, it's likely that the two contributors that haven't responded will be as big a problem for the effort to loosen the license as the people who have actively expressed a desire to keep some sort of copyleft.

You're right that `releaseLocks` should release this but the problem is that it is never called since I believe that the thread that would call it hangs at `_checkBackend` on line 918, waiting for the other thread to release the lock. For the same reason that we don't commit .pyc files to master. These files are not the preferred form for modification or even readable. Verifying a PR that modifies these files is therefore impossible. These files are the output of an automatic process and so keeping them around is just wasted space. It probably wouldn't be such a big deal to me except that the kinto-admin plugin is by far the heaviest thing in a git clone, weighing in at 7MB (out of 7.9MB), so doing a `git pull` is much slower when someone updates these files.

I agree that master should be "runnable", but I don't think that means we need to keep kinto-admin build outputs in source control. We could agree that "runnable" means "runnable without the admin", or we could agree that "runnable" means "runnable with everything in a non-optimized way", or we could agree that "runnable" means "runnable once you execute a build step". Each of these ways could permit kinto-admin build outputs to not be checked in to git, because they don't belong there.

Great !

I think we can make it even simpler. If we want a command to get started quickly, then we don't need to ask for advanced stuff like uwsgi.

If some settings are defined in the .ini file, then Kinto will use the default values automatically. So as a start, we can start with the bare minimum.
See, for example, the one we use to run the load tests here : https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/blob/master/loadtests/server.ini

I also suggest that we ask for the backend only once, and use the same for all of them. For example, if I say that I want `postgresql` then postgresql is used for `storage` `cache` and `permission`. This way we don't prompt for so many things, and it helps to get started quickly !

In order to guide you, I propose this list of small tasks:

* [ ] Remove the files ending with `~`
* [ ] Remove the duplicated `__init__.py` file in `scripts/` folder
* [ ] Remove prompts for advanced configuration values to keep it to the bare minimum (like the one in load tests)
* [ ] Ask for the backend values only once and use the same in all of them

After that, we will make sure it works, we will improve it to be able to run with commandline arguments, we will document it, add more settings and advanced stuff etc. But first we have to reduce it the bare minimum that works and make it look shiny :)

Good luck !

The training algorithm is not meant to run optimally. It selects a random action with probability `random_eps` and adds some noise to the action, `noise_eps`.

These perturbations help training by helping the agent explore, but generally prevent the training success rate from ever reaching 100 percent. That is why the code includes an evaluation rollout worker in which both of these values are set to 0 so that the agent can achieve much higher success rate without random actions penalizing it.

### ContinuousGoalOrientedMoveToPoint-v0 Environment
#### Env Summary:
The goal of this environment is to move the agent (a red dot) to touch the goal (a greev dot). The agent has momentum and will bounce off of walls if it touches one.
#### Gif of Environment
Please excuse the inconsistent frame rate, I was controlling the agent with a keyboard and did not hit the keys at a constant frequency.
![goal_oriented_move_to_point gif](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3925736/41128422-f90d9854-6a62-11e8-9f86-ac1b72b820b9.gif)
#### Env Details
State Space: Positions in a unit square, velocities between -0.05 and +0.05.
Goal Space: Any point in the unit square, re-sampled each episode
Initial State Space: A point in the unit square, re-sampled each episode as well as a velocity within the velocity bounds re-sampled each episode
Action Space: X force component, Y force component, between -1 and 1
Reward: 1 for reaching goal, 0 otherwise
Terminal Condition: When the goal is achieved or when the time limit is reached (time limit comes from wrapper)
#### Source code
[continuous_goal_oriented_particle.py.gz](https://github.com/openai/gym/files/2082209/continuous_goal_oriented_particle.py.gz)
Make sure to register the environment with a time limit.
```
from gym.envs.registration import register

def register_move_to_point_env():
	register(
		id='ContinuousGoalOrientedMoveToPoint-v0',
		entry_point='baselines.her.experiment.continuous_goal_oriented_particle:ContinuousGoalOrientedMoveToPoint',
		max_episode_steps=250,
		reward_threshold=1,)
```

A small example of using this from @vbardiovskyg's code snippet:

```python
SEQ_LENGTH = 5
EMBEDDING_DIM = 50

with tf.Graph().as_default() as g:
  
  embed_layer = hub.Module(
    f"https://tfhub.dev/google/nnlm-en-dim{EMBEDDING_DIM}-with-normalization/1", 
    trainable=False, name='text_embedding')
  
  sentences = tf.placeholder(dtype=tf.string, shape=(None, SEQ_LENGTH))
  batch_size = tf.shape(sentences)[0]
  
  flat_sentences = tf.reshape(sentences, [-1])

  embeddings = embed_layer(flat_sentences)
  
  sentence_embedding = tf.reshape(embeddings, 
                                  [batch_size, SEQ_LENGTH, EMBEDDING_DIM])

  with tf.Session(graph=g) as sess:
    sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())
    sess.run(tf.tables_initializer())

    output = sess.run(sentence_embedding, feed_dict={
        sentences: [
            "cat is on the mat".split(), 
            "dog is in the fog".split(), 
            "padded sentence UNK UNK UNK".split()]
    })
    
    print(output.shape)
# (3, 5, 50)
```

You'd want better handling of padding/trimming sequences, but I normally do that with the `tf.data` API

[As we describe in the README](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard#is-my-data-being-downsampled-am-i-really-seeing-all-the-data), TensorBoard downsamples data to preserve memory usage. You should apply the following patch if you want TensorBoard to retain more images:
```diff
diff --git a/tensorboard/backend/application.py b/tensorboard/backend/application.py
index e6e9540..3a19903 100644
--- a/tensorboard/backend/application.py
+++ b/tensorboard/backend/application.py
@@ -45,7 +45,7 @@ from tensorboard.plugins.core import core_plugin
 
 DEFAULT_SIZE_GUIDANCE = {
     event_accumulator.COMPRESSED_HISTOGRAMS: 500,
-    event_accumulator.IMAGES: 10,
+    event_accumulator.IMAGES: 1000,
     event_accumulator.AUDIO: 10,
     event_accumulator.SCALARS: 1000,
     event_accumulator.HISTOGRAMS: 50,
```
Thanks for the quick fix!

> I think we want to keep the max height for now because of internal issues like this:

Indeed, this makes sense when the dashboard is not in actual-size mode. But it's not relevant in the actual-size case: the user's image is actually 1×320, and thus it will appear. (To be clear: I'm _only_ referring to the case where actual-size mode is active; when that's not the case it absolutely makes sense to have a `max-height`, be it of 1024px or just the 330px to match the card width.)

> it's easy to miss the small images that reside in the gaps,

I see your point, though I'm not convinced; even a 1×1 will require at least the ~350px of space for its enclosing card—a card that has a colored border that draws the eye. In the case where images are small (e.g., MNIST), forcing one-image-per-line increases the vertical scroll amount by a factor of ~5, and further devalues [small multiples](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Small_multiple). But I'll leave this one up to you.

* csvjson unless --stream and --no-inference are set but --skip-lines is not
* csvlook
* csvpy
* csvsort
* csvsql
* csvstat

> CategoricalEncoder (TODO: check on Joris' recent work in sklearn here)

I picked up again this work last week, and I think the design should rather fixed now (https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/9151/). For now, we opted to only provide two ways to encode: just replacing with integer (categorial 'codes') and one-hot / dummy encoding (as there are many ways one could do 'categorical encoding'). Feedback is always welcome there!

Note that you might not want to follow the design exactly, as it does not use anything dataframe-specific (it can handles dataframes, but does not take advantage of it). Eg, the categories are specified as a positional list for the different columns, not as a dict of column name -> categories.

> All transformers should take an optional columns argument. When specified, the transformation will be limited to just columns (e.g. if doing a standard scaling, and columns=['A', 'B'], only 'A' and 'B' are scaled). By default, all columns are scaled

In scikit-learn, we are currently taking another route: instead of adding such a `columns` keyword to all transformers, I am working on a `ColumnTransformer` to perform such column-specific transformers (https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/9012).

The example from above:

```
make_pipeline(
    StandardScaler(columns=[0, 1]),
    DummyEncoder(columns=[2, 3])
)
```

would then become

```
make_pipeline(
    make_column_transformer(
        ([0, 1], StandardScaler()),
        ([2, 3], DummyEncoder())
    ),
    ... regresson/classifier
)
```

Maybe a bit less easy from user point of view, but it means that the transformers itself don't need to be updated to handle column selection (and since it is in a single object, it would naturally share the reading of X).  
But of course, that doesn't you need to adapt the same pattern here.
To be clear, I'd love to see mrocklin/sparse expand, or be merged into Scipy when stable. I just don't think I can do it alone. :)

Unfortunately I don't have time for a detailed review. Just a few comments after glancing over:
- The exponent used for the density computation is actually a hyperparameter of the algorithm described in the paper. In our implementation, it defaults to the number of features, but can be set arbitrarily by the user.
- In the original implementation, `k_neighbors` is decreased when it is larger than the number of samples in a given cluster. This enables the user to specify, for example, `k_neighbors=float('inf')` to apply SMOTE to interpolate among all samples of a cluster.
- This implementation does not encompass limit cases of SMOTE and random oversampling described in section 3.2 of the paper.

Anymail has no way of knowing where the settings came from -- it's all just a single django.conf.settings by the time it gets to Anymail's code.

It's impossible to say what's not working without seeing the code you're using to set Anymail settings in local_settings.py and settings.py. (And we'd also need to know whether the local_settings import is before or after any other Anymail settings you might be defining in settings.py.)

The way you're importing local_settings will overwrite any settings defined above it in settings.py. And any settings defined below that import in settings.py will overwrite the local_settings. 

If you have an `ANYMAIL = {...}` dict in both files, whichever dict comes last will *completely overwrite* the other one -- there's no magic in python's import mechanism that could merge them. (And if that's the problem, you can use the non-dict `ANYMAIL_MAILGUN_API_KEY = ...` form to work around it.)
Fix released in Anymail v3.0.

@jagerman Thanks for having a look at this. I thought about your comment for a bit, and here's my opinion for what it's worth:
- There's a reason why `pybind11/stl.h` isn't included automatically by `pybind11.h`, as it introduces possibly unwanted (and perhaps even arguably confusing) container conversion behavior. In some sense, `pybind11/stl_bind.h` is an alternative to  `pybind11/stl.h` that uses api binds instead of conversion. We should be careful about keeping this distinction clear, if/when we go ahead with the header re-organisation you proposed. Perhaps `bind/stl.h` and `cast/stl.h`?
- I might be wrong, but I feel like `std::variant` and `std::optional` are in a bit of a different situation. Similarly to `std::tuple`/`std::pair` etc, their behavior appears to be totally fine imho, and perhaps they should just be included automatically by `pybind11.h`, like `std::tuple`/`std::pair`.
- I agree with the reorganization but there are diminishing returns. In particular, I believe that reorganizing api headers (those that enable different combinations of features) is way more important than reorganizing `detail` internals. It also seems more important than breaking down headers into various individual feature headers `pybind11/stl/*.h` etc as you also suggested, since I find it unlikely that these would be individually included (most users would just use the meta-header).

Hi @po2xel,

the issue you point out occurs when an object is converted into a handle which should contain a new reference (i.e. with a refcount that is too large by 1). This is a rare situation that mainly comes up in the type casters, and it's a good thing if it is not easy to do so by accident. The ``object``-based types (which includes ``none``) decrease the reference count upon destruction, which . This design is how all of pybind11 works, and it is very much intended.

Saving the bootstrap version to disk and including the bootstrap script name are great ideas. As a minor point, since the built version of `certbot-auto` doesn't have a great way to tell where different pieces of code come from, I think we should include the name of the bootstrap function instead but I think this has the same effect. With this and our IRC discussion in mind, I propose the updated plan:

* Add a version number to each bootstrap script that must be incremented when new OS dependencies are added. This will be something like `DEB_BOOTSTRAP_VERSION`.
* Take the giant `if [ -f /etc/debian_version ]` logic out of `Bootstrap()`. Instead, this `if` `else` tree will execute on every run and set two variables. First, it will set `Bootstrap` to the function to run to install OS dependencies on this system. Second, it will set `BOOTSTRAP_VERSION` to `"<bootstrap function name> <bootstrap version number>"`. If the user set `--no-bootstrap`, `Bootstrap()` will be a noop and `BOOTSTRAP_VERSION` will be a dummy value.
* Write a function that returns the bootstrap version stored on disk. If the file doesn't exist, it will return something like "Unknown 0".
* When creating the virtualenv, write a file containing the current bootstrap version named something like "certbot-bootstrap-version.txt". The assumption here is when coupled with the code below, if we're creating the venv, we just bootstrapped (or we were ran with `--no-bootstrap`).
* Update the beginning of [phase 2](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/98805ccae01205537cbfa48dfa3704982fc128dc/certbot-auto#L658) to look something like:
```
# If the venv doesn't exist and we're in phase 2, we just bootstrapped so don't do it again
if [ -d "$VENV_PATH" -a "$BOOTSTRAP_VERSION" != $(SavedBootstrapVersion) ]; then
  # noninteractive mode or stdin and stdout are connected to a terminal
  if [ $ASSUME_YES = 1 -o -t 0 -a -t 1]; then
    rm -rf "$VENV_PATH"
    exec "$0" "$@"
  else
    echo Skipping upgrade because new OS dependencies need to be installed.
    # Set INSTALLED_VERSION to be the same so we don't update the venv
    INSTALLED_VERSION=$LE_AUTO_VERSION
  fi
elif [ -f "$VENV_BIN/letsencrypt" ]; then
...

> Having a single instance or even all concurrently running Certbot instances fail isn't a big deal in my opinion. We tell people to run the cronjob twice a day and this advice is followed by our package maintainers so I think a single failure due to lock contention hardly matters at all. In fact, if people have e-mail from cron set up properly this will help notify them that there is a problem and there are two cron jobs trying to run Certbot on their system.

This is actually exactly what worries me -- if a job runs at the same time, it's probably not from randomness, it's because they were both set up to run at the same time, and will continue to do so consistently. So they'll continue failing in the exact same way each time. And I'm not comfortable relying on having cron email set up.

That being said, maybe only one of the two completing successfully is fine... except obviously for the ones where it's always the wrong cron job that wins out, which could totally happen if it starts like 30 seconds before the other one, and certbot always takes longer than that to complete. Oh and even worse, in the cases where (possible since we have multiple locks), neither job completes successfully.

> If we block and have more than one lock (including any additional locks in the plugins), we have ensure the locking order never changes or risk deadlock. While we could definitely do this, I'm not sure it's worth the extra complexity and risk of bugs.

I definitely hear this, especially given that we're trying to protect people from an edge case to begin with. I just worry that for the exact people we're trying to protect, we'll accidentally end up failing them even more.

> Putting a lockfile in /tmp though allows arbitrary users to circumvent or abuse these protections. They can acquire the lock themselves and prevent Certbot from running and renewing your certs. They can delete the lockfile after it's created removing these protections entirely. While maybe neither of these things are a big deal, it opens Certbot up to other problems that we do not have with another approach.

Ugh. You're totally right about these. These are new problems that we'd be opening Certbot up to. `/tmp` doesn't solve our problems.


What really worries me is going from a "maybe would have worked fine" to a "definitely always breaks things for this set of users" due to an update we push. And the "both jobs fail" case is the worst, I think. If that's not possible, I'd be much more comfortable with this. So question: how likely is that to happen? How do we handle threading? What about plugins? Are our instructions always actually run in order?

We already have a daemon monitoring thread that touches a status file during the execution of tasks. This `pulse` thread let sos know that the task is running and will terminate as soon as the main task is terminated. We should perhaps implement a mechanism to let the pulse thread kill the main job when it receives an instruction to stop the task.

The advantage of this method is that we do not need to worry about how the task is started (e.g. through PBS on a worker). We just need to touch a status file, let the pulse thread know something is wrong, and it will kill the process.

One exception I can think of is docker when the pulse thread might not be able to stop the docker instance. This is related to #460 and we might be able to achieve this by using another pulse thread.

More usefully, we might be able to "pass the pulse thread to action" while the action is executing, and return the pulse thread to the master process when an action is done....

The global remote feature is actually not difficult to implement but I do not know how well it plays with other  parts of SoS. For example, the `remote` design requires **all file operations to be handled in tasks** which might or might not be a huge problem.

I can answer a few questions of yours:

> First, who am I to decide which convention is right for a project to use with Tox?

Seriously, it looks like you're a much more considerate and careful person than quite a few other folks, especially from the Tox project. This is certainly a good thing, but fear not. Pick a [beautiful solution](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/) and let us get a :+1: from, say, @RonnyPfannschmidt, @obestwalter, and others. Then nobody will complain.

> In your example, you have used `djangoXX` for the env names, whereas I have preferred to use `djXX`. In the case of the Python variants, it's Tox that has chosen, not me

Whatever helps the Python eco-system to get [more readable](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/) and self-explaining configuration files and code. `pyXX` was certainly also not the most obvious choice, but we have now gotten used to it, and to translating it to `pythonX.X`.

My [example from above](https://github.com/tox-dev/tox-travis/issues/77#issuecomment-336852502) was to show a logical naming scheme that could be applied quite straight-forward to every framework that has a `<name>` and a semantic `<Major><minor>` version, to get translated to `NAME=M.m` as an environment value.

> which frameworks need to have this shortcut

There are PRs for this. I'd start with Django, because I'm interested in it. (I could argue, it's the most popular framework. But let's have the community decide via PRs.)

In the end, it should really be just a list of accepted names (if at all). With the naming scheme explained above there is no maintenance nightmare to be expected.

> I totally agree that Travis' configuration for excludes and environments is unfortunately verbose, but I don't see a great way around it.

If there is a way, technically, it would be cool to have a minimum setup (e.g. just the Python versions) and have them be split up into jobs according to the Tox configuration automagically, when `tox-travis` is operating.

That would be nice. Really nice.

Actor fault tolerance actually is implemented in the legacy backend, but the behavior is to reconstruct the actor from the start (and replay all of its tasks), which isn't always possible if there are no resources available (e.g., GPUs) and it may take too long to be practical. There is support for checkpointing as well, but it is experimental.

The new approach introduces exceptions at the application level so that applications can decide how to handle actor failures, which should be more practical. Ultimately we will probably combine elements of these approaches.
